Ayahs

الٓمٓ (١)
Alif, Lam, Meem. (1)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (٢)
These are the signs of the wise Book, (2)
هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣)
a guidance and mercy for the virtuous, (3)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (٤)
who maintain the prayer, pay the zakat, and are certain of the Hereafter. (4)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٥)
Those follow their Lord’s guidance, and it is they who are the felicitous. (5)

Content of Surah Luqman

It is well-known and famous among commentators that this Surah was revealed in Makkah, although some, such as Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, have excepted a few verses of this Surah, like the fourth verse which speaks of prayer and zakat; or like Fakhr Razi who, in addition to this verse, excepted verse 27 which discusses the vast knowledge of the Lord. However, there is no clear evidence for these exceptions, because prayer and zakat (of course, zakat in a general sense) also existed in Makkah, and the account explaining the vastness of the Lord's knowledge is not something that bears the signs of being Medinan.

Therefore, Surah Luqman, by virtue of being Makkan, contains the general content of Makkan Surahs; that is, it discusses the fundamental Islamic beliefs, especially "the Beginning" (Tawhid) and "the Hereafter" (Ma'ad), as well as "Prophethood."

In general, the content of this Surah can be summarized into five sections:

The first section, after mentioning the Disconnected Letters (Muqatta'at), refers to the greatness of the Quran and its being a guidance and mercy for the believers who possess specific qualities. In contrast, it speaks of those who show such stubbornness and persistence against these verses as if their ears are deaf; furthermore, they strive to divert others from the Quran by creating unhealthy amusements.

The second section speaks of the signs of Allah in the creation of the heavens and their establishment without any pillars, the creation of mountains on the earth, various living creatures, the descent of rain, and the growth of plants.

The third section, on this occasion, relates a portion of the wise sayings of Luqman—that man of God—during his counsel to his son, which begins with monotheism and the struggle against polytheism, and ends with recommendations for kindness to parents, prayer, enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, patience in the face of hardships, cheerfulness with people, humility, modesty, and moderation in affairs.

In the fourth section, it returns once again to the proofs of monotheism, speaking of the subjugation of the heavens and the earth, the abundant blessings of the Lord, and the reproach of the logic of idolaters who fell into this wilderness of misguidance solely based on imitating their ancestors; it extracts from them an admission regarding the issue of the Lord's Creatorship, which is the foundation of His servitude.

It also unveils the vast and endless knowledge of Allah by mentioning a clear example, and in this regard, in addition to mentioning the "horizonal" signs (ayat al-afaqi), it discusses in an interesting manner the primordial monotheism (fitri) whose manifestation occurs when one is caught amidst the waves of calamity.

The fifth section contains a brief and soul-shaking reference to the issue of the Hereafter and life after death; it warns man not to be deceived by the life of this world and to think of that eternal abode.

It completes this subject by mentioning a glimpse of the Lord's knowledge of the Unseen—that He is aware of everything related to man, including the moment of his death and even the fetus in the mother's womb—and thus ends the Surah.

Incidentally, it is clear that the naming of this Surah as "Surah Luqman" is because of the significant and profound discussion of Luqman's counsels mentioned in this Surah, and it is the only Surah that speaks of this wise man.

Virtue of Surah Luqman

Several narrations regarding the virtue of this Surah have been related from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and some of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, including: In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Luqman, Luqman will be his companion on the Day of Resurrection, and he will be given ten rewards (hasanat) equal to the number of those who have performed good deeds or committed evil deeds (by virtue of the principle of enjoining good and forbidding evil regarding them)." [1] And in another Hadith from Imam Baqir عليه السلام we read:

"Whoever recites Surah Luqman at night, Allah appoints thirty angels to protect him during his night from Iblis and his soldiers until morning; and if he recites it during the day, these thirty angels will protect him from Iblis and his soldiers until evening." [2] We have said many times, and we say again, that all this virtue, reward, and honor for reciting a Surah of the Quran is because recitation is a prelude to reflection and thought, and that in turn is a prelude to action; one should not expect all this virtue solely through the mere movement of the tongue.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, vol. 8, p. 312. [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 193.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الم (1) تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ (2) هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ (3) الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ هُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ (4) أُولئِكَ عَلى‌ هُدىً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ (5)

Commentary

Who are the Doers of Good?

This Surah begins by mentioning the greatness and importance of the Quran, and the mention of the Disconnected Letters at its beginning is also a subtle reference to this reality: that these verses, which are composed of simple letters of the alphabet, possess such great and sublime content that they completely transform the destiny of human beings (Alif, Lam, Mim).

Therefore, after mentioning the "Disconnected Letters," He says: "These are the verses of the Wise Book" (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ).

"Tilka" in the Arabic language is used for pointing to the distant, and we have repeatedly said that this expression is specifically a metaphor for the greatness and importance of these verses—as if they are situated at the peak of the heavens and at a distant point! Describing the "Book" as "Wise" (Hakim) is either because of the firmness of its content, as falsehood never finds its way into it, and it distances itself from every kind of superstition, saying nothing but the truth and inviting to nothing but the path of truth; it stands exactly opposite to "idle talk" (lahw al-hadith) which appears in the following verses.

Or it means that this Quran is like a wise scholar who, while silent, speaks with a thousand tongues, teaches, counsels, encourages, warns, relates exemplary stories, and in short, possesses wisdom in every sense of the word; and this opening has a direct correlation with the words of "Luqman the Wise" discussed in this Surah.

Of course, there is no obstacle to both meanings of "wisdom" being intended in the above verse.

The next verse explains the ultimate goal of the Quran's revelation with this phrase: "This Wise Book is a guidance and mercy for the doers of good" (هُدىً وَ رَحْمَةً لِلْمُحْسِنِينَ).

"Guidance" is in fact a prelude to the "Mercy of the Lord," because man first finds the truth in the light of the Quran's radiance, believes in it, and applies it in his actions, and following that, he becomes subject to the vast mercy and endless blessings of the Lord.

It is noteworthy that here the Quran is considered a source of guidance and mercy for the "Muhsinin" (doers of good), while at the beginning of Surah An-Naml, it is a source of guidance and glad tidings for the "Mu'minin" (believers) (هُدىً وَ بُشْرى‌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

And at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, it is a source of guidance for the "Muttaqin" (the God-fearing) (هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ).

This difference in expression may be because without piety (taqwa) and God-fearingness, the spirit of submission and acceptance of truths is not awakened in man, and naturally, there will be no guidance.

And once we pass this stage of accepting the truth, the stage of faith (iman) arrives, where in addition to guidance, there will also be glad tidings of divine blessings.

And if we go beyond the stage of piety and faith and reach the stage of righteous action, Allah's mercy is also added there.

Therefore, the three verses above recount three successive stages of the stages of perfection for the servants of Allah: the stage of accepting the truth, the stage of faith, and the stage of action; and the Quran in these three stages is respectively a source of "guidance," "glad tidings," and "mercy" (reflect on this).

The next verse describes the "Muhsinin" with three attributes, saying: "They are those who establish prayer, give zakat, and they have certainty in the Hereafter" (الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ هُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ).

Their connection with the Creator through prayer, and with Allah's creation through zakat, is definitive; and their certainty in the court of Resurrection is a powerful motive for avoiding sin and for performing duties.

And in the last verse under discussion, He states the end and outcome of the "Muhsinin" as such: "They are upon guidance from their Lord, and they are the successful ones" (أُولئِكَ عَلى‌ هُدىً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ).

The phrase "أُولئِكَ عَلى‌ هُدىً مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ" shows on one hand that their guidance is guaranteed by their Lord, and on the other hand, the expression "ala" (upon) is evidence that it is as if guidance is a swift mount for them, and they are riding and in control of this mount.

From here, the difference between this "guidance" and the guidance mentioned at the beginning of the Surah becomes clear, because the first guidance is the readiness to accept the truth, and this guidance is the program for reaching the destination.

Incidentally, the phrase "أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ" which, according to Arabic grammar, indicates exclusivity (hasr), shows that the only way to salvation is this very path: the path of the doers of good, the path of those who are in connection with Allah and Allah's creation, and the path of those who have complete faith in the Beginning and the Hereafter.

Ayahs

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْتَرِى لَهْوَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّخِذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٦)
Among the people is he who buys diversionary talk that he may lead [people] astray from Allah’s way without any knowledge, and he takes it in derision. For such there is a humiliating punishment. (6)
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا وَلَّىٰ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا كَأَنَّ فِىٓ أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْرًا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٧)
When Our signs are recited to him he turns away disdainfully, as if he had not heard them [at all], as if there were a deafness in his ears. So inform him of a painful punishment. (7)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٨)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, for them will be gardens of bliss, (8)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٩)
to remain in them [forever]—a true promise of Allah, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (9)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have stated that the first verses under discussion were revealed concerning "Nadr bin al-Harith."

He was a merchant who used to travel to Persia, and during his travels, he would learn the stories of the Persians and recount them to the Quraysh. He would say: "If Muhammad tells you the stories of 'Ad and Thamud, I will tell you the stories of Rostam and Esfandiyar, and the news of Khosrow and the Persian kings!" People would gather around him and abandon listening to the Quran.

Others have said that this part of the verses was revealed concerning a man who had purchased a singing slave-girl who would sing for him day and night, making him heedless of the remembrance of Allah.

The late great commentator, "Tabarsi," after mentioning this occasion of revelation, says:

A hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard confirms the above occasion of revelation, as he صلى الله عليه وآله said:

لا يحل تعليم المغنيات و لا بيعهن، و اثمانهن حرام و قد نزل تصديق ذلك فى كتاب اللَّه: "وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ‌ ..."

"It is not permissible to teach singing slave-girls, nor to sell them, and their price is haram (forbidden). The confirmation of this has been revealed in the Book of Allah: 'And of the people is he who purchases idle talk...'"

Commentary

Singing, One of the Great Snares of the Devils!

In these verses, the discussion is about a group that stands in direct contrast to the "Muhsinin" (the doers of good) and the "Believers" mentioned in the previous verses.

Here, the talk is of a group that employs its capital for vanity and for leading people astray, thereby purchasing misery for themselves in this world and the Hereafter!

First, He says: "And of the people is he who purchases idle talk to mislead [others] from the way of Allah without knowledge" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ).

"And who takes it [the way of Allah] in ridicule" (وَ يَتَّخِذَها هُزُواً) [1]. At the end of the verse, He adds: "Those will have a humiliating punishment" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ).

Purchasing idle talk (lahw al-hadith) occurs either by literally buying superstitious and false legends with money, as we read in the story of "Nadr bin al-Harith."

Or it is through purchasing singing slave-girls to arrange gatherings of vanity and falsehood, as mentioned in the hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the occasion of revelation.

Or it refers to spending wealth in any form or manner to achieve this illegitimate goal—namely, idle and vain speech.

It is astonishing that these blind-hearted individuals would purchase vain matters at the highest price, yet they ignored the Divine verses and wisdom that the Lord provided to them for free.

There is also the possibility that "purchasing" (ishtira') here is used metaphorically, referring to any effort or endeavor to reach this objective.

[1]: The pronoun in "yattakhidhaha" refers back to "the verses of the Book" mentioned in the preceding verses. Some have also suggested it refers to "Sabil" (the Way); the word "Sabil" is sometimes treated as masculine and sometimes as feminine in the verses of the Quran.

As for "Lahw al-Hadith," it has a broad and extensive meaning that encompasses any kind of speech or melodies that are distracting and cause heedlessness, leading a person toward vanity or misguidance. This includes "Ghina" (sinful singing), lustful tunes, and melodies, as well as speech that leads a person toward vanity and corruption not through melody, but through its content.

Or it may be through both ways, as is common in the lyrics and romantic poems of ordinary singers, where both the content is misleading and the melody is as well! Or like superstitious stories and myths that cause people to deviate from the Divine "Straight Path."

Or it may refer to mocking words intended to erase the truth and weaken the foundations of faith, similar to what is narrated about Abu Jahl and his companions. He would turn to the Quraysh and say: "Do you want me to feed you from the 'Zaqqum' with which Muhammad threatens us?!" Then he would send for "butter and dates" to be brought and say: "This is that very Zaqqum! ..." In this way, he would take the Divine verses in ridicule.

In any case, "Lahw al-Hadith" has a broad meaning that encompasses all these and their like. If Islamic narrations or the words of commentators point to one of these specifically, it is by no means evidence for the exclusivity or limitation of the verse's concept.

In the hadiths that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, expressions are found that demonstrate this breadth of the word's meaning:

Among them, we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

الغناء مجلس لا ينظر اللَّه الى اهله، و هو مما قال اللَّه عز و جل "وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يَشْتَرِي لَهْوَ الْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌"

"A gathering of Ghina (sinful singing) is a gathering upon whose people Allah does not look (and does not include them in His grace), and it is an instance of what Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, said: 'And of the people is he who purchases idle talk to mislead [others] from the way of Allah...'" [1]

The use of the expression "Lahw al-Hadith" instead of "al-Hadith al-Lahw" (vain speech) perhaps indicates that their primary goal is the vanity and idleness itself, and the speech is merely a means to reach it.

The phrase "to mislead from the way of Allah" also has a broad meaning that includes both ideological misguidance—as we read in the stories of Nadr bin al-Harith and Abu Jahl—and moral deviation, as mentioned in the hadiths regarding Ghina.

The expression "without knowledge" indicates that this misguided and deviant group does not even believe in their own false religion; rather, they merely follow ignorance and blind imitation. They are ignorant ones who entrap others in their own ignorance.

This is the case if we consider "without knowledge" as a description for the misleaders. However, some commentators have also suggested it could be a description for the "misled"—meaning they lead ignorant and unaware people into the valley of deviation and falsehood unknowingly.

These unaware individuals sometimes go even further; they are not satisfied with just the entertaining and distracting aspects of these matters, but rather they make their idle talk a means for ridiculing and mocking the Divine verses. This is what is pointed out at the end of the verse: "And who takes it in ridicule."

As for describing the punishment as "Muhin" (humiliating and insulting), it is because the penalty must match the crime. They insulted the Divine verses, so Allah

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, p. 228 (Chapter on the Prohibition of Ghina).

has also designated a punishment for them that is not only painful but also insulting.

The next verse points to the reaction of this group toward the Divine verses, and in fact, compares it with their reaction toward "idle talk," saying: "And when Our verses are recited to him, he turns away arrogantly as if he had not heard them, as if there was in his ears deafness" and he hears no speech at all (وَ إِذا تُتْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ آياتُنا وَلَّى مُسْتَكْبِراً كَأَنْ لَمْ يَسْمَعْها كَأَنَّ فِي أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْراً).

And at the end, it recounts the painful retribution for such a person: "So give him tidings of a painful punishment" (فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ).

The expression "he turns away arrogantly" indicates that his turning away is not merely because of interference with his worldly interests and desires; rather, it goes beyond that, and the motive of arrogance and pride against Allah and His verses—which is the greatest sin—exists in his action.

It is interesting that He first says: "He shows such indifference to the Divine verses as if he had not heard them at all and passes by them with complete disregard," then He adds: "Not only does he not hear these verses, it is as if he is entirely deaf and hears no speech at all!" The recompense for such individuals is also proportionate to their deeds; just as their actions were painful for the people of truth, Allah has made their punishment painful and will afflict them with a "painful punishment" (al-alim).

It is also necessary to note that the use of the word "Bashshir" (give tidings) regarding the painful Divine punishment is appropriate for the conduct of the arrogant ones who took the Divine verses in ridicule, and those like Abu Jahl who interpreted the "Zaqqum of Hell" as butter and dates!

In the following verses, the discourse returns to the state of the true believers, with whom this comparison began, so that it may also conclude with them. He says: "Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds, for them are the Gardens of Pleasure" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ النَّعِيمِ). Yes, this group—unlike the faithless arrogant ones and the blind-hearted misleaders who neither see the signs of Allah in the world nor hear the words of Allah's messengers with the ear of the soul—by the decree of the awakened intellect, the seeing eye, and the hearing ear that Allah has granted them, both believe in the Divine verses and implement them in their righteous deeds. How striking it is that those had "painful punishment" and these have "Gardens of Pleasure."

More importantly, these bountiful heavenly gardens are eternal for them: "Abiding therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها). "This is the certain promise of Allah, a promise that cannot be broken" (وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقًّا).

And Allah neither gives false promises nor is He incapable of fulfilling His promises, for "He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise" (وَ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ).

It is worth noting that regarding the arrogant ones, "punishment" is mentioned in the singular, while for the righteous believers, "Gardens" is mentioned in the plural, because Allah's mercy always precedes His wrath.

The emphasis on eternity (khulud) and the "true promise" of Allah is also an emphasis on this prevalence of "mercy" over "wrath."

"Na'im," which comes from the root "Ni'mah" (bounty/blessing), has a broad meaning that includes all types of material and spiritual blessings, even those blessings that are incomprehensible to us who are imprisoned in the jail of the body in this world. "Raghib" says in "Al-Mufradat": "Na'im" means "abundant blessing" (النعيم النعمة الكثيرة).

Key Points

1- The Prohibition of Ghina

Undoubtedly, Ghina (sinful singing) is generally forbidden according to the majority of Shi'a scholars, and this fame reaches the level of consensus and agreement.

Many Sunni scholars have also emphasized this view, although some of them have allowed certain exceptions, and perhaps some of these exceptions are not actually exceptions but are considered outside the subject of Ghina (technically known as takhassus).

"Qurtubi," under the verses being discussed, says in this regard: "The singing common among a group of people, whenever it includes romantic poems about women and descriptions of their beauty, and mentions of wine and other prohibited things, all scholars are in agreement on its prohibition, for it is an instance of 'idle talk' and censured singing. However, if it is free from these matters, a small amount of it is permissible during festivals and wedding celebrations, as well as for providing energy during difficult tasks, as mentioned in Islamic history regarding the digging of the Trench, or the poems that 'Anjashah' used to recite to stimulate the camels during the caravan's journey to Mecca in the Farewell Pilgrimage. But what is common today among the 'Sufis' who use various musical instruments in this regard is forbidden." [1]

What Qurtubi mentioned as exceptions, such as reciting "Huda" (a specific chant) for camels or the specific poems that Muslims recited rhythmically while digging the Trench, most likely was not and is not part of Ghina at all; it is similar to the poems that groups recite with a specific rhythm in marches or religious celebration and mourning gatherings.

In Islamic sources, there are many proofs for the prohibition of Ghina, including the aforementioned verse: "And of the people is he who purchases idle talk..." and some other verses of the Quran which, at the very least according to the narrations regarding their interpretation, have been applied to Ghina, or Ghina has been counted as one of their instances.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that in interpreting the verse "And avoid false speech" (Hajj-30), he said:

قول الزور الغناء:

"False speech is Ghina." [2] Likewise, the same Imam عليه السلام said in interpreting the verse "And those who do not witness falsehood" (Furqan-72): "What is meant by it is Ghina." [3] Regarding the interpretation of this very verse under discussion, numerous narrations have been narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and Imam Ali bin Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, which identify Ghina as one of the instances of "idle talk" that leads to "humiliating punishment." [4] In addition to these, many other narrations—aside from the interpretation of verses—are found in Islamic sources that emphatically state the prohibition of Ghina.

In a hadith narrated from "Jabir bin Abdullah" from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

كان ابليس اول من تغنى: "Satan was the first person who sang Ghina." [5]

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 7, p. 5136. (2, 3, 4, 5): Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, pp. 225-227, 231 (Chapter 99 on the Prohibition of Ghina).

And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

بيت الغناء لا- تؤمن فيه الفجيعة، و لا تجاب فيه الدعوة، و لا يدخله الملك:

"A house in which there is Ghina is not safe from sudden death and painful calamity, prayers are not answered in it, and angels do not enter it." [1] And in another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read:

الغناء يورث النفاق، و يعقب الفقر:

"Ghina breeds the spirit of hypocrisy and brings about poverty and misery." [2] In another hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, among those he counted as cursed and far from the mercy of Allah is the female singer, the one who pays her a wage, and the one who consumes that money! (المغنية ملعونة، و من اداها ملعون، و آكل كسبها ملعون) [3] In famous Sunni sources as well, numerous narrations have been narrated in this regard.

Among them is a narration mentioned in "Durr al-Manthur" from a large group of traditionists from Abi Umamah from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who said:

لا يحل تعليم المغنيات و لا بيعهن و اثمانهن حرام

"Teaching singing women is not permissible, nor is their sale or purchase, and the price taken for them is also forbidden." [4] A similar meaning has been narrated by the author of "Al-Taj" from Tirmidhi and Imam Ahmad (Al-Taj, Vol. 5, p. 287).

"Ibn Mas'ud" narrates from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

الغناء ينبت النفاق فى القلب كما ينبت الماء البقل:

"Ghina grows the spirit of hypocrisy in the heart just as water grows plants!" [1] Altogether, the narrations narrated in this regard are so numerous that they reach the level of tawatur (successive transmission), and for this reason, most Islamic scholars have issued fatwas on its prohibition. In addition to the Shi'a scholars who are almost unanimous in this statement, its prohibition has also been narrated from Abu Hanifa. When Ahmad, the famous leader of the Sunnis, was asked about Ghina, he replied: "It grows the spirit of hypocrisy in a person!"

And Malik, another leader of the Sunnis, said in response to this question: "Only the transgressors (fasiqun) do it!" And "Shafi'i" explicitly stated that the testimony of the practitioners of Ghina is not accepted, and this itself is evidence of their transgression (fisq).

It has also been narrated from the companions of Shafi'i that they consider Shafi'i's fatwa to be one of prohibition, contrary to what some have imagined. [2]

2- What is Ghina?

There is not much difficulty regarding the prohibition of Ghina; the difficulty lies in identifying the subject of Ghina.

Is every pleasant and beautiful voice Ghina?

Certainly not, for it has come in Islamic narrations and the practice of Muslims also indicates that the Quran, the Adhan, and the like should be recited with a pleasant and beautiful voice.

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse being discussed. [2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verses being discussed.

Is Ghina every sound in which there is "Tarji'" (vibration of the voice in the throat, or so-called trilling)? That too is not proven.

What can be gathered from the collective words of the jurists and the statements of the Sunnis in this regard is that Ghina refers to melodies that are provocative of pleasure (tarab-angiz), vain, and false.

In clearer terms: Ghina refers to melodies that are suitable for gatherings of debauchery, sin, and corruption.

In yet another expression: Ghina is said of a sound that stimulates the lustful powers in a human being, such that the person feels that if wine and sexual corruption were present alongside that sound, it would be perfectly fitting!

It is also noteworthy that sometimes a "melody" is itself Ghina, vain, and false, as well as its content—such as when romantic and corruption-inducing poems are sung with provocative melodies. At other times, only the melody is Ghina—such as when poems with profound content or verses of the Quran, supplications, and litanies are sung with a melody suitable for gatherings of hedonists and transgressors. In both cases, it is forbidden (take note).

It is also necessary to mention that sometimes two types of meanings are mentioned for Ghina: a "general meaning" and a "specific meaning." The specific meaning is what we mentioned above—namely, melodies that stimulate lusts and are suitable for gatherings of debauchery.

However, the general meaning is any kind of beautiful sound. Therefore, those who have interpreted Ghina in the general sense have divided it into two types: "permissible Ghina" and "forbidden Ghina."

What is meant by forbidden Ghina is what was mentioned above, and what is meant by permissible Ghina is a beautiful and pleasant sound that does not induce corruption and is not suitable for gatherings of debauchery.

Therefore, there is almost no disagreement on the principle of the prohibition of Ghina; the disagreement is in the manner of its interpretation.

Of course, "Ghina" also has doubtful instances (like all other concepts) where a person truly does not know whether a certain sound is suitable for gatherings of debauchery or not. In this case, by the decree of the "Principle of Innocence" (Asl al-Bara'ah), it is judged as permissible (of course, after sufficient awareness of the conventional meaning of Ghina according to the above definition).

From here it becomes clear that heroic sounds and melodies suitable for battlefields or sports or the like have no reason for their prohibition.

Of course, regarding Ghina, there are other discussions as well, such as some exceptions that some have proposed and others have denied, and other issues that should be discussed in books of jurisprudence.

The final word we deem necessary to mention here is that what we said above pertains to singing; however, the use of musical instruments and their prohibition is another discussion that is outside the scope of this topic.

3- The Philosophy of the Prohibition of Ghina

Careful consideration of the concept of "Ghina" with the conditions mentioned in the explanation of its subject clearly reveals the philosophy of its prohibition.

In a brief review, we encounter the following harms:

A: Encouragement of Moral Corruption

Experience has shown—and experience is the best witness—that many individuals, under the influence of Ghina melodies, abandon the path of piety and godliness and turn toward lusts and corruption.

A gathering of Ghina is usually a center for various types of corruption, and what fuels these corruptions is Ghina itself.

In some reports found in foreign newspapers, we read that in a gathering where a group of girls and boys were present, and a specific Ghina melody was performed, such excitement overcame the girls and boys that they attacked one another and committed many atrocities that the pen is ashamed to mention.

In the commentary "Ruh al-Ma'ani," a statement is narrated from one of the leaders of the "Umayyads" who said to them: "Avoid Ghina, for it diminishes modesty, increases lust, destroys personality, becomes a substitute for wine, and does exactly what intoxication does." [1] This shows that even they had realized its harms.

And if we see that it is repeatedly mentioned in Islamic narrations that Ghina grows the spirit of hypocrisy in the heart, it is an allusion to this very reality. The spirit of hypocrisy is the spirit of being tainted with corruption and distancing oneself from piety and godliness.

Also, if it is mentioned in narrations that angels do not enter a house in which there is Ghina, it is because of this taint of corruption, for angels are pure and seek purity, and they loathe these polluted environments.

B: Becoming Heedless of the Remembrance of Allah

The expression "Lahw" (vanity/diversion) used in the interpretation of "Ghina" in some Islamic narrations refers to this very reality: that Ghina makes a person so intoxicated with lusts that it makes them heedless of the remembrance of Allah.

In the verses above, we read that "Lahw al-Hadith" is one of the factors of "deviation" from the "Way of Allah" and causes "painful punishment."

[1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 21, p. 60.

In a hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read:

كل ما الهى عن ذكر اللَّه فهو من الميسر:

"Everything that makes one heedless of the remembrance of Allah (and causes one to sink into lusts) is in the ruling of gambling." [1]

C: Harmful Effects on the Nerves

Ghina and music are, in fact, among the important factors that anesthetize the nerves. In other words, narcotics sometimes enter the body through the mouth and drinking (like wine).

Sometimes through smelling and the sense of olfaction (like heroin).

Sometimes through injection (like morphine).

And sometimes it is through the sense of hearing (like Ghina).

For this reason, sometimes Ghina and specific melodies put individuals into such a state of euphoria that a condition similar to intoxication overcomes them. Of course, sometimes it does not reach this stage, but it nonetheless creates a mild anesthesia.

For this reason, many of the harms of narcotics exist in Ghina, whether its anesthesia is mild or severe.

"Careful attention to the biographies of famous musicians shows that during their lives, they gradually suffered from mental distress, to the point that they slowly lost their nerves; some became afflicted with psychological illnesses, some lost their senses and departed to the land of madness, some became paralyzed and weak, and some, while playing music, had their blood pressure rise and suffered sudden strokes." [2]

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 12, p. 235. [2]: The Effect of Music on the Soul and Nerves, p. 26.

In some books written regarding the harmful effects of music on the human nerves, the states of a group of famous musicians and singers are mentioned who, while performing a program, were afflicted by strokes and sudden death, losing their lives in that very gathering. [1] In short, the harmful effects of Ghina and music on the nerves to the point of producing madness, and on the heart and blood pressure, and other undesirable stimulations are such that they do not require much discussion.

From the statistics prepared on mortality in our era, it is gathered that sudden deaths have increased significantly compared to the past. Various factors have been mentioned for this increase, including the increase of Ghina and music on a global level.

D: Ghina as a Tool of Colonialism

The world's colonialists have always feared the awakening of the people, especially the younger generation. For this reason, part of their extensive programs for continuing colonialism is to plunge societies into heedlessness, ignorance, and unawareness, and to spread various types of unhealthy entertainment.

Today, narcotics do not only have a commercial aspect; they are an important political tool—that is, colonial policies. The establishment of centers of prostitution, gambling clubs, and other unhealthy entertainments, including the expansion of Ghina and music, is one of the most important tools they insist upon to anesthetize the thoughts of the people. For this reason, a major part of the time of the world's radio stations is comprised of music, and this subject is among the primary programs of the mass media.

[1]: Refer to the same previous source, p. 92 onwards.

Ayahs

خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ (١٠)
He created the heavens without any pillars that you may see, and cast firm mountains in the earth lest it should shake with you, and He has scattered in it every kind of animal. And We sent down water from the sky and caused every splendid kind [of plant] to grow in it. (10)
هَـٰذَا خَلْقُ ٱللَّهِ فَأَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۚ بَلِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (١١)
This is the creation of Allah. Now show Me what others besides Him have created. Indeed, the wrongdoers are in manifest error! (11)

Commentary

This is the Creation of Allah; What Have Others Created?

Following the discussion regarding the Quran and faith in it in the preceding verses, the two verses under discussion speak of the evidences of Monotheism (Tawhid), which is another of the most fundamental principles of belief.

In the first verse, it points to five parts of the Lord's creation that have an inseparable bond with one another (the creation of the heavens and the suspension of celestial bodies in space, the creation of mountains to maintain the stability of the earth, then the creation of living creatures, and after that, water and plants which are the means of their nourishment).

It says: "He created the heavens without pillars that you can see" (خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَها).

"Amad" (rhyming with qamar) is the plural of "amud", meaning "pillar." Restricting it with the phrase "that you can see" (tarawnaha) is evidence that the heavens do not have visible pillars. The implication of this statement is that it does have pillars, but they are not visible. As we have previously mentioned in the commentary of Surah Ar-Ra'd, this expression is a subtle reference to the laws of attraction and repulsion (gravity), which, like a very powerful yet invisible pillar, hold the celestial bodies in their places.

In a tradition narrated by Husayn ibn Khalid from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, this meaning is explicitly stated. He said: "Glory be to Allah! Does Allah not say: 'without pillars that you can see'?" I said: "Yes." He then said: "There are pillars, but you do not see them!" [^1] and [^2] In any case, the above sentence is one of the scientific miracles of the Holy Quran, the further explanation of which we provided under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd (Volume 10, page 110).

Then, regarding the philosophy of the "creation of mountains," it says: "And He cast into the earth firm mountains lest it should sway with you" (وَ أَلْقى‌ فِي الْأَرْضِ رَواسِيَ أَنْ تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ). [^3] This verse, which has many parallels in the Quran, shows that mountains are the means of the earth's stability. Today, from a scientific perspective, this truth is also proven—that mountains contribute to the earth's stability in several ways:

  • From the perspective that their roots are interconnected and, like a strong armor, protect the globe against the pressures arising from internal heat. If they were not there, destructive earthquakes would be so frequent that they might not give humans a chance to live.
  • From the perspective that this firm crust resists the pressure of the gravitational pull of the moon and the sun. If the mountains were not there, a massive tide would occur in the earth's soil crust, not unlike the tides of the seas, making life impossible for humans.
  • And from the perspective that they break the pressure of storms and reduce the friction of the air adjacent to the earth during its rotational movement to a minimum. If they were not there, the surface of the earth, like dry deserts, would be the scene of deadly storms and crushing winds throughout the day and night. [^4]

Now that the blessing of the tranquility of the heavens has been provided by its invisible pillars, and the tranquility of the earth by the mountains, it is the turn for the creation of living beings and their peace, so that they may step into the arena of life in a calm environment. It says: "And He scattered therein every [kind of] moving creature" (وَ بَثَّ فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ دَابَّةٍ).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, vol. 2, p. 278. [^2]: Those who have taken the above verse as evidence for the absolute negation of pillars are forced to assume a transposition in the verse and say it was originally: "He created the heavens, you see them without pillars," and this is certainly contrary to the apparent meaning. [^3]: "Tamid" is from the root "mayd" (rhyming with sayd), meaning the swaying and agitation of massive objects. The phrase an tamida bikum is understood as li-alla tamida bikum (lest it should sway with you). [^4]: For more explanation regarding the benefits of mountains, please refer to Volume 10, page 113 onwards.

The expression "every [kind of] moving creature" (min kulli dabbah) refers to the diversity of life in its various forms—from creatures so small they cannot be seen with the naked eye and fill our entire environment, to giant creatures whose magnitude fills man with awe.

Likewise, creatures of different colors and completely different forms—aquatic and aerial, birds and reptiles, various insects, and the like—each of which has a world of its own, reflecting the phenomenon of life in hundreds of thousands of mirrors.

However, it is clear that these creatures need food and water; therefore, in the subsequent phrases, it refers to these two subjects, saying: "And We sent down water from the sky and caused to grow therein every noble kind [of plant]" (وَ أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ).

In this way, it describes the foundation of the life of all creatures, especially humans, which consists of water and plants—a spread table with diverse foods across the entire earth, each of which, from the perspective of creation, is a proof of the greatness and power of the Lord.

It is noteworthy that in describing the creation of the first three parts, the verbs are mentioned in the third person (He), but when it reaches the issue of the descent of rain and the cultivation of plants, the verbs are expressed in the first person (We), saying: "We sent down water from the sky, and We caused plants to grow in the earth."

This is itself one of the techniques of eloquence—that when mentioning various matters, they are expressed in two or more diverse forms so that the listener does not feel any sense of fatigue. Furthermore, this expression shows that the descent of rain and the cultivation of plants are subjects of special attention.

This verse once again refers to "duality (pairs) in the world of plants," which is also one of the scientific miracles of the Quran. At that time, duality (the existence of male and female genders) in the world of plants had not been widely proven, and the Quran unveiled it (further explanation on this matter can be found in Volume 15 under verse 7 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara).

Additionally, describing the pairs of plants as "noble" (karim) refers to the various blessings and benefits found within them.

After mentioning the greatness of Allah in the world of creation and the various aspects of creation, the discourse turns to the polytheists and calls them to account, saying: "This is the creation of Allah; so show Me what those besides Him have created!" (هذا خَلْقُ اللَّهِ فَأَرُونِي ما ذا خَلَقَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ).

Certainly, they could not claim that any of the creatures of this world were created by idols. Therefore, they confessed to the Monotheism of Creation (Tawhid al-Khaliqiyyah). Despite this, how could they justify polytheism in worship? For the Monotheism of Creation is proof of the Monotheism of Lordship (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah) and the oneness of the Manager of the universe, which in turn is proof of the Monotheism of Worship (Tawhid al-Ubudiyyah).

Therefore, at the end of the verse, it characterizes their action as oppression and deviation, saying: "Nay, but the wrongdoers are in manifest error" (بَلِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ).

We know that "oppression" (zulm) has a broad meaning that includes placing anything in other than its proper place. Since the polytheists attributed worship and sometimes the management of the world to idols, they committed the greatest oppression and deviation.

Furthermore, the above expression is a subtle reference to the connection between "oppression" and "deviation," because when a person does not recognize the position of objective beings in the world, or recognizes it but does not observe it and does not see everything in its proper place, this oppression will certainly cause his deviation and error.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا لُقْمَـٰنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ (١٢)
Certainly We gave Luqman wisdom, saying, ‘Give thanks to Allah; and whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake. And whoever is ungrateful, [let him know that] Allah is indeed all-sufficient, all-laudable.’ (12)
وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَـٰنُ لِٱبْنِهِۦ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُۥ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ (١٣)
When Luqman said to his son, as he advised him: ‘O my son! Do not ascribe any partners to Allah. Polytheism is indeed a great injustice.’ (13)
وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ فِى عَامَيْنِ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَيْكَ إِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٤)
We have enjoined man concerning his parents: His mother carried him through weakness upon weakness, and his weaning takes two years. Give thanks to Me and to your parents. To Me is the return. (14)
وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُشْرِكَ بِى مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا ۖ وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا ۖ وَٱتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَىَّ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٥)
But if they urge you to ascribe to Me as partner that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. Keep their company honourably in this world and follow the way of those who turn to Me penitently. Then to Me will be your return, whereat I will inform you concerning what you used to do. (15)

Commentary

Respecting Parents

In alignment with the preceding discussions regarding Monotheism (Tawhid), polytheism (Shirk), the significance and greatness of the Quran, and the wisdom employed within this Heavenly Book, the verses under discussion and several following them speak of "Luqman the Wise." They recount a portion of the important counsels of this man of God concerning Monotheism and the struggle against polytheism, as well as the significant moral issues reflected in Luqman’s advice to his son.

These ten counsels, expressed over six verses, beautifully articulate matters of belief, the principles of religious duties, and ethical discourses.

As for who "Luqman" was and what his characteristics were, we shall discuss this in the "Key Points" section, God willing. However, suffice it to say here that evidence suggests he was not a prophet, but rather a refined and self-disciplined man who emerged victorious in the struggle against his base desires (hawa al-nafs), and Allah had opened the springs of knowledge and wisdom within his heart.

Regarding the greatness of his status, it is enough that Allah placed his counsels alongside His own words and mentioned them within the verses of the Quran. Indeed, when a human heart is illuminated by the light of wisdom through purity and piety, Divine words flow from his tongue; he speaks as Allah speaks and thinks as Allah approves! With this brief introduction, we return to the commentary of the verses.

In the first verse, He says: "And We certainly gave Luqman wisdom, [saying], 'Be grateful to Allah.' And whoever is grateful is grateful for [the benefit of] himself. And whoever denies [His favor]—then indeed, Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا لُقْمَانَ الْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ حَمِيدٌ) [1]. Regarding what "wisdom" (hikmah) is, it must be noted that many meanings have been mentioned for it, such as "recognizing the secrets of the universe," "awareness of the truths of the Quran," "attaining the truth in speech and action," and "the gnosis and recognition of Allah."

However, all these meanings can be gathered together to define the wisdom the Quran speaks of and which Allah bestowed upon Luqman as: "A collection of gnosis and knowledge, pure ethics, piety, and the light of guidance."

In a hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام, we read that in explaining this verse to Hisham ibn al-Hakam, he said: "What is meant by wisdom is understanding and intellect." [2]

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators as to whether the phrase "أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ" (Be grateful to Allah) has something implied (muqaddar) or not. Some believe that the phrase "We said to him" is implied before it, while others say there is no need for an implication, and "an" in the phrase "an ishkur" is explanatory (tafsiriyyah), because gratitude itself is the essence of wisdom and wisdom is the essence of it (both interpretations are acceptable). [2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 13 (Book of Intellect and Ignorance, Hadith 12).

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of this verse: "He was given the recognition of the Imam of his time" (أوتي معرفة إمام زمانه). [1] It is clear that each of these is considered one of the branches of the broad concept of wisdom and they are not contradictory.

In any case, because Luqman possessed this wisdom, he engaged in thanking his Lord. He knew the purpose of Divine blessings and their application, and he utilized them correctly for the very purpose they were created. Indeed, wisdom is exactly this: "Applying everything in its proper place." Therefore, "gratitude" and "wisdom" return to a single point.

The verse also mentions the result of "gratitude" and "ingratitude" for blessings in this way: "Gratitude for blessings is to the benefit of the person himself" and "ingratitude for blessings is to his own detriment," because Allah is independent of everyone. If all possible beings were to be grateful, nothing would be added to His greatness, and "if all created beings become disbelievers, not a speck of dust would settle on the hem of His Majesty!" The "lam" in the phrase "أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ" is the lam of specification (ikhtisas), and the "lam" in "li-nafsihi" is the lam of benefit. Therefore, the benefit of gratitude—which is the continuity of the blessing and its increase, plus the reward of the Hereafter—returns to the person himself, just as the harm of ingratitude only affects him.

The expression "Free of need and Praiseworthy" (Ghaniyyun Hamid) indicates that a grateful person, when dealing with ordinary people, either gives something to the bestower of the blessing or, if he gives nothing, raises the bestower's status in the eyes of people by praising him. However, in the case of Allah, neither of these two meanings applies. He is independent of all, and He is worthy of the praise of all praisers. The angels recite His praise, and every particle of existence is busy with His glorification and praise. If a human being shows ingratitude with his spoken tongue, it has the least effect, for even the particles of his own being are busy with His praise and glorification through their state of being! It is noteworthy that "gratitude" (shukr) is expressed with the "present/future tense" (mudari'), which indicates continuity and persistence, while "disbelief/ingratitude" (kufr) is expressed with the "past tense" (madi), which applies even to a single instance. This suggests that ingratitude even once can bring painful consequences, whereas gratitude must be continuous and persistent for a person to traverse his path of perfection.

After introducing Luqman and his status of knowledge and wisdom, the verse points to his first counsel, which is simultaneously the most important recommendation to his son, stating: "And [recall] when Luqman said to his son while he was advising him, 'O my son, do not associate [anything] with Allah. Indeed, association [with Him] is a great injustice'" (وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَانُ لِابْنِهِ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُ يَا بُنَيَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ).

Luqman’s wisdom necessitates that, before anything else, he addresses the most fundamental issue of belief, which is the issue of "Monotheism" (Tawhid). Monotheism in all fields and dimensions, because every destructive and anti-Divine movement stems from polytheism—from world-worship, status-worship, desire-worship, and the like, each of which is considered a branch of polytheism.

Just as the foundation of all correct and constructive movements is Monotheism—attaching the heart only to Allah, submitting to His command, severing ties from other than Him, and smashing all idols at the threshold of His Majesty! It is noteworthy that Luqman the Wise mentions the reason for rejecting polytheism as being that polytheism is a "great injustice," using an expression that contains emphasis from several aspects. [1] And what injustice is greater than this, which is committed against Allah by placing a worthless being as His equal, against the creation of Allah by leading them astray and subjecting them to oppression through criminal acts, and against oneself by falling from the peak of the honor of servitude to the Lord into the depths of the valley of the humiliation of worshipping other than Him!

The next two verses are, in fact, parenthetical statements expressed by Allah in the midst of Luqman’s counsels. However, "parenthetical" does not mean unrelated, but rather Divine words that have a clear connection with Luqman’s words. In these two verses, the discussion is about the blessing of the existence of parents, their hardships, services, and rights, and placing gratitude to parents alongside gratitude to "Allah." Furthermore, it is considered an emphasis on the sincerity of Luqman’s counsels to his son, because parents, with such profound affection and sincerity of intention, could not possibly express anything in their counsels except the good and welfare of the child.

First, He says: "And We have enjoined upon man [care] for his parents" (وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ بِوَالِدَيْهِ).

Then, He points to the extraordinary hardships of the mother, saying: "His mother carried him, [increasing her] in weakness upon weakness" (حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُ وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ). [2]

[1]: "Inna," the "lam," and the "nominal sentence structure" are each means of emphasis. [2]: The phrase "وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ" (weakness upon weakness) might be a "circumstantial clause" (hal) for "mother" by implying the word "possessor of" (dhat). It is also possible that it is an "absolute object" (maf'ul mutlaq) for an implied verb from the root "w-h-n."

This matter has been scientifically proven, and experience has also shown that mothers suffer from weakness and frailty during pregnancy, because they dedicate the essence of their lives and the marrow of their bones to the nourishment of their fetus, offering the best of all the vital substances of their existence to it.

For this reason, mothers during pregnancy suffer from deficiencies in various vitamins, which, if not compensated, cause ailments for them. This even continues during the period of lactation and breastfeeding, because "milk is the essence of the mother's life."

Therefore, He adds immediately after: "And his weaning is in two years" (وَفِصَالُهُ فِي عَامَيْنِ).

As is also pointed out elsewhere in the Quran: "Mothers shall suckle their children for two whole years" (وَالْوَالِدَاتُ يُرْضِعْنَ أَوْلَادَهُنَّ حَوْلَيْنِ كَامِلَيْنِ) (Al-Baqarah: 233).

Of course, the intention is the complete period of breastfeeding, although it may sometimes be less than that. In any case, the mother during these 33 months (the period of pregnancy and the period of breastfeeding) performs the greatest sacrifice both spiritually and emotionally, as well as physically and in terms of services for her child.

It is interesting that at the beginning, He makes a recommendation regarding both parents, but when describing the hardships and services, He focuses on the hardships of the mother to make the human being aware of her sacrifices and her great right. Then He says: "I commanded: 'Be grateful to Me and to your parents'" (أَنِ اشْكُرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيْكَ).

Be grateful to Me, for I am your Creator and your primary Benefactor, and I have given you such kind parents; and also be grateful to your parents, who are the intermediaries of this grace and are responsible for transferring My blessings to you.

And how beautiful and meaningful it is that gratitude to parents is placed exactly alongside gratitude to Allah.

At the end of the verse, with a tone not devoid of threat and reproach, He says: "To Me is the destination" (إِلَيَّ الْمَصِيرُ).

Yes, if you fall short here, all these rights, hardships, and services will be examined there and accounted for down to the last detail. You must be able to answer the Divine accounting regarding gratitude for His blessings, as well as regarding gratitude for the blessing of the existence of parents and their pure, unadulterated affections.

Some commentators have noted here that in the Holy Quran, the emphasis on observing the rights of parents appears repeatedly, but recommendations regarding children are seen less frequently (except in the case of the prohibition of killing children, which was an exceptional, sinister, and ugly custom in the Age of Ignorance).

This is because parents, by virtue of their powerful emotions, are less likely to forget their children, whereas it is often seen that children forget their parents, especially in old age and infirmity, and this is the most painful state for them and the worst ingratitude for the children. [1]

Since the recommendation for goodness toward parents might create the illusion for some that one must compromise even in the matter of beliefs, disbelief, and faith, He adds in the next verse: "But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge (at least), do not obey them" (وَإِنْ جَاهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰ أَنْ تُشْرِكَ بِي مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا). Never should the relationship between a person and his parents take precedence over his relationship with Allah, and never should kinship emotions dominate his ideological conviction.

The expression "endeavor" (jahadaka) indicates that parents sometimes, thinking they want the child's happiness, strive and struggle to pull him toward their deviant belief, and this is seen in all parents.

The duty of children is never to surrender to these pressures, to maintain their intellectual independence, and not to exchange the belief in Monotheism for anything.

Furthermore, the phrase "that of which you have no knowledge" (مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ) points to the fact that if we were to hypothetically ignore the proofs for the falsehood of polytheism, there is at least no proof to establish it, and no excuse-seeker can provide a reason to prove polytheism.

Beyond this, if polytheism were a reality, there would have to be a proof to establish it, and since there is no proof to establish it, that itself is a proof of its falsehood.

Again, since this command might create the illusion that one should act with severity and disrespect toward polytheistic parents, He immediately adds that lack of obedience to them in the matter of disbelief and polytheism is not a reason for an absolute break in relations with them; rather, at the same time, "accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness" (وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِي الدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا).

In terms of the world and material life, treat them with love, affection, and gentleness, but in terms of belief and religious programs, do not surrender to their thoughts and suggestions. This is exactly the central point of moderation where the rights of Allah and the rights of parents are combined.

Therefore, He adds afterward: "And follow the path of those who turn to Me" (وَاتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَيَّ)—the path of the Prophet and the true believers.

Because after that, "to Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do" and will reward or punish accordingly (ثُمَّ إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

The successive negations and affirmations, and the commands and prohibitions in the above verses, are so that Muslims find the main line in such matters where, at first glance, a conflict is imagined between performing two necessary duties, and place themselves on the correct path without the slightest excess or deficiency. This precision and delicacy of the Quran in these details is one of the aspects of its deep eloquence and clarity.

In any case, the above verse is completely similar to what appears in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 8, which says: "And We have enjoined upon man goodness to his parents. But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them. To Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do." (وَوَصَّيْنَا الْإِنْسَانَ بِوَالِدَيْهِ حُسْنًا وَإِنْ جَاهَدَاكَ لِتُشْرِكَ بِي مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا إِلَيَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِمَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

And regarding it, a "circumstance of revelation" (shan al-nuzul) has been narrated in some commentaries, which we mentioned under verse 8 of Surah Al-Ankabut.

Key Points

1- Who was Luqman?

The name Luqman appears in two verses of the Quran in this very Surah. There is no explicit proof in the Quran as to whether he was a prophet or only a wise individual. However, the tone of the Quran regarding Luqman indicates that he was not a prophet, because regarding prophets, there is usually mention of messengership, calling toward Monotheism, struggling against polytheism and the deviations of the environment, not demanding a wage or reward, and also giving glad tidings and warnings to nations. Meanwhile, regarding Luqman, none of these matters are mentioned; only his counsels, which were expressed privately to his son—though their content has a general aspect—are mentioned. This is evidence that he was only a wise man.

In a hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Truly I say, Luqman was not a prophet, but he was a servant who reflected much, had excellent certainty, loved Allah, so Allah loved him and bestowed wisdom upon him..." (حقا اقول لم يكن لقمان نبيا، و لكن كان عبدا كثير التفكر، حسن اليقين، احب الله فاحبه و من عليه بالحكمة ...).

It is mentioned in some histories that Luqman was a black slave from the people of Sudan in Egypt, and despite an unattractive face, he had an enlightened heart and a purified soul. From the very beginning, he spoke the truth, did not taint trust with betrayal, and did not interfere in matters that did not concern him. [1] Some commentators have suggested the possibility of his prophethood, but as we said, there is no proof for it; rather, we have clear evidence against it.

In some narrations, it is mentioned that a person said to Luqman: "Were you not a shepherd with us?" He replied: "Yes, it is so." The questioner asked: "Then from where did all this knowledge and wisdom become your lot?" He replied: "The decree of Allah, fulfilling trusts, truthfulness in speech, and silence regarding what does not concern me" (قدر الله، و اداء الامانة و صدق الحديث و الصمت عما لا يعنينى). [1]

In the continuation of the narration mentioned above from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is also stated: "One day Luqman was sleeping in the middle of the day for rest, when suddenly he heard a voice saying: 'O Luqman! Do you want Allah to make you a vicegerent (khalifah) on earth so that you may judge between people with truth?' Luqman replied to that voice: 'If my Lord gives me the choice, I accept the path of well-being ('afiyah) and do not submit to this great trial! But if He commands, I accept His command with my soul, for I know that if He places such a responsibility on my shoulders, He will certainly help me and protect me from slips.' The angels—while he did not see them—said: 'O Luqman, why?' He said: 'Because judging between people is the most difficult of stations and the most important of stages, and waves of injustice and oppression are directed toward it from every side. If Allah protects a person, he is worthy of salvation, and if he takes the path of error, he has deviated from the path of Paradise. One who is humble in the world and honorable in the Hereafter is better than one who is honorable in the world and humble in the Hereafter; and one who chooses the world over the Hereafter will not reach the world and will also lose the Hereafter!' The angels were amazed by Luqman’s interesting logic. Luqman said this and fell asleep, and Allah cast the light of wisdom into his heart. When he woke up, he began to speak with wisdom!..." [1]

2- A Glimpse of Luqman’s Wisdom

Some commentators, in accordance with Luqman’s counsels reflected in the verses of this Surah, have recounted parts of the wise sayings of this man of God, a summary of which we bring here:

A: Luqman would say to his son: "O my son! Indeed, the world is a deep sea, and many people have perished in it. So make your ship in it faith in Allah, make its sail reliance upon Allah, and make your provision in it the piety of Allah. If you are saved, it is by the mercy of Allah, and if you perish, it is because of your sins!" (يا بنى! ان الدنيا بحر عميق، و قد هلك فيها عالم كثير، فاجعل سفينتك فيها الايمان بالله، و اجعل شراعها التوكل على الله، و اجعل زادك فيها تقوى الله، فان نجوت فبرحمة الله و ان هلكت فبذنوبك!). [2]

This same matter is narrated in the book Al-Kafi, within the words of Imam al-Kazim عليه السلام to Hisham ibn al-Hakam, in a more complete form from Luqman the Wise: "O my son, indeed the world is a deep sea in which a great many have drowned. So let your ship in it be the piety of Allah, its cargo faith, its sail reliance, its captain the intellect, its guide knowledge, and its rudder patience." (يا بنى ان الدنيا بحر عميق، قد غرق فيها عالم كثير، فلتكن سفينتك فيها تقوى الله، و حشوها الايمان، و شراعها التوكل، و قيمها العقل، و دليلها العلم، و سكانها الصبر). [1]

B: In another discourse to his son regarding the etiquette of travel, he says: "My son! When you travel, take your weapon, clothes, tent, water-drinking vessel, sewing tools, and necessary medicines that both you and your companions can use, and be in harmony with your fellow travelers in everything except in disobedience to Allah. My son! When you travel with a group, consult with them in your affairs. Smile in their faces. Be generous with the provisions you have. When they call you, answer, and if they seek help from you, help them. Observe silence as much as you can. Pray much. Be generous with your mount, water, and food. If they seek testimony to the truth from you, testify. If they seek counsel from you, strive to obtain a sound opinion and do not answer without sufficient thought and reflection; use all your power of thinking for the answer to the consultation, for whoever does not express his most sincere opinion in response to a consultation, Allah takes away from him the blessing of discernment and thought. When you see your companions walking and striving, rise and strive with them. Listen to the command of one who is older than you. If they have a legitimate request from you, always give a positive answer and never say 'no,' for saying 'no' is a sign of weakness and a cause for blame... Never delay the prayer from its prime time, and pay this debt of yours immediately. Pray in congregation, even if you are in the most difficult of states... If you can, before eating any food you want to eat, spend some of it in the way of Allah. Recite the Book of Allah, and do not forget the remembrance of Allah." [1]

C: This story is also famous regarding Luqman: when he was working as a slave for his master, one day the master said to him: "Slaughter a sheep for me and bring me the two best parts of it." He slaughtered a sheep and brought its tongue and heart. A few days later, he gave him the same command, but said: "Bring me the two worst parts of it." Luqman again slaughtered a sheep and brought the same tongue and heart. The master was surprised and asked about this. Luqman replied: "The heart and the tongue, if they are pure, are better than anything, and if they become impure, they are more wicked and worse than anything!" [2]

We end this discourse with a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. He said: "By Allah, the wisdom that was granted to Luqman from the Lord was not because of his lineage, wealth, beauty, or body. Rather, he was a man who was strong and powerful in performing the command of Allah, avoided sins and ambiguities, was silent and quiet, observed with precision, thought much, was sharp-sighted, and never slept during the (early) day, nor did he lean (in the manner of the arrogant) in gatherings, and he fully observed etiquette. He did not spit, did not play with anything, and was never seen in an inappropriate state... He never saw two people in a state of dispute except that he reconciled them, and if he heard a good word from someone, he would certainly ask for the source of that word and its explanation. He sat and associated much with the jurists and scholars... He sought sciences through which he could overcome base desires, treated his soul with the power of thought, reflection, and constant lessons, and only went after work that was to his benefit (in religion or the world). He never interfered in matters that did not concern him, and for this reason, Allah bestowed wisdom upon him." [1]

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 196. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 316, under the verse discussed. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 1, p. 13 (Book of Intellect and Ignorance). [1]: Same source. [2]: Tafsir al-Baydawi and al-Tha'labi; however, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan only narrates the first part of Luqman’s words. [1]: Majma' al-Bayan (summarized).

Ayahs

يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنَّهَآ إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُن فِى صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَوْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ (١٦)
‘O my son! Even if it should be the weight of a mustard seed, and [even though] it should be in a rock, or in the heavens, or in the earth, Allah will produce it. Indeed Allah is all-attentive, all-aware. (16)
يَـٰبُنَىَّ أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱنْهَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَكَ ۖ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ (١٧)
O my son! Maintain the prayer and bid what is right and forbid what is wrong, and be patient through whatever may befall you. That is indeed the steadiest of courses. (17)
وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ (١٨)
Do not turn your cheek away disdainfully from the people, and do not walk exultantly on the earth. Indeed Allah does not like any swaggering braggart. (18)
وَٱقْصِدْ فِى مَشْيِكَ وَٱغْضُضْ مِن صَوْتِكَ ۚ إِنَّ أَنكَرَ ٱلْأَصْوَٰتِ لَصَوْتُ ٱلْحَمِيرِ (١٩)
Be modest in your bearing, and lower your voice. Indeed the ungainliest of voices is the donkey’s voice.’ (19)

Commentary

Stand Like a Mountain and Treat People with Kindness!

Luqman’s first counsel pertained to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism (Shirk). His second counsel concerns the accounting of deeds and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), which completes the link between the "Origin" and the "Return."

He says: "O my son! If there be (a deed) even the weight of a mustard seed, and it be lodged in a rock, or in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth (on the Day of Judgment) and settle its account; for Allah is Subtle and Aware" (يَا بُنَيَّ إِنَّهَا إِنْ تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُنْ فِي صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ أَوْ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا اللَّهُ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ).

"Mustard seed" (Khardal) refers to a plant with very tiny black seeds, which is used as a proverb for smallness and insignificance.

This is an indication that good and evil deeds, no matter how small or insignificant, and no matter how hidden or concealed—like a mustard seed hidden inside a rock in the depths of the earth or in a corner of the heavens—Allah, the Subtle and Aware, who is cognizant of all beings, small and large, minor and major, throughout the entire universe, will bring it forth for accounting, reward, and punishment. Nothing is lost in this system! The pronoun in "innaha" (إِنَّهَا) refers to "good deeds, evil deeds, and actions." [^1]

[^1]: Some have suggested that the aforementioned pronoun is the "pronoun of the case" (damir al-shan), or that it refers to the concept of Shirk, but both possibilities are remote.

Mindfulness of this awareness of the Lord regarding human actions, and the preservation of all virtues and vices in the book of Divine knowledge—and the fact that nothing is destroyed in this universe—is the essence of all individual and social reforms. It is a powerful driving force toward good deeds and an effective deterrent against evils and vices.

The mention of "heavens" and "earth" after the mention of "rock" is, in fact, a case of mentioning the general after the specific.

In a hadith narrated in Usul al-Kafi from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "Beware of the belittled sins, for they have a seeker. One of you says, 'I sinned and I seek forgiveness,' while Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, says: 'We record that which they have sent before and their footprints, and all things We have enumerated in a clear register' (36:12). And the Almighty and Sublime said: 'If there be (a deed) even the weight of a mustard seed, and it be lodged in a rock, or in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth; for Allah is Subtle and Aware.'" [^1]

After strengthening the foundations of the Origin and the Return, which are the basis of all ideological beliefs, he addresses the most important of actions, namely the issue of prayer (Salah), saying: "O my son! Establish the prayer" (يَا بُنَيَّ أَقِمِ الصَّلَاةَ).

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 204.

For prayer is your most important link with the Creator; prayer awakens your heart, purifies your soul, and illuminates your life. It washes the traces of sin from your soul, radiates the light of faith in the sanctuary of your heart, and restrains you from indecency and evil.

After the program of prayer, he addresses the most important social command, namely "Enjoining the Good and Forbidding the Evil," saying: "Invite people to virtues and the recognized good, and restrain them from vices and indecencies" (وَأْمُرْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَانْهَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ).

And after these three important practical commands, he addresses the issue of patience and steadfastness—which is to faith as the head is to the body—saying: "And be patient over whatever befalls you (of calamities and difficulties), for this is of the steadfast heart of things and the fundamental duties of every human" (وَاصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا أَصَابَكَ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ الْأُمُورِ).

It is certain that in all social endeavors, especially in the program of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil, there are numerous difficulties. Dominance-seeking opportunists and self-centered, tainted sinners do not surrender easily; they may even resort to harassment and accusing those who enjoin the good and forbid the evil. Without patience, steadfastness, and endurance, one can never triumph over these difficulties.

"Azm" (عزم) means a firm will, and the expression "Azm al-Umur" (عزم الأمور) here either means matters that have been emphatically commanded by the Lord, or matters regarding which a human must have an iron will and a resolute decision. Whichever of these two meanings it may be, it points to its great importance.

The expression "dhalika" (ذٰلِكَ) refers to patience and endurance, though there is also the possibility that it refers to all the matters mentioned in the verse, including prayer and enjoining the good and forbidding the evil. However, in some other verses of the Quran, this expression appears after the issue of patience, which strengthens the first possibility.

Then Luqman addresses moral issues in relation to people and oneself. First, he recommends humility, modesty, and a pleasant demeanor, saying: "And turn not your cheek away from people with indifference" (وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ). "And walk not insolently upon the earth" (وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا).

"For Allah loves not any arrogant boaster" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ).

"Tusa'ir" (تُصَعِّرْ) is derived from the root "sa'ar" (صعر), which originally refers to a type of disease that affects camels, causing them to twist their necks.

"Marah" (مَرَحًا) means pride and intoxication resulting from blessings.

"Mukhtal" (مُخْتَالٍ) is from the root "khayal" (خيال), and "khuyla" (خيلاء) refers to someone who sees themselves as great based on a series of imaginations and fancies.

"Fakhur" (فَخُورٍ) is from the root "fakhr" (فخر), meaning someone who boasts to others (the difference between "mukhtal" and "fakhur" is that the former refers to arrogant mental imaginations, while the latter refers to external arrogant actions).

In this way, Luqman the Wise points here to two very ugly and undesirable traits that cause the disintegration of sincere social relations: one is arrogance and indifference, and the other is pride and self-conceit. Both share the commonality of plunging a person into a world of illusion, fancy, and self-superiority, severing their connection with others.

Especially considering the linguistic root of "sa'ar," it becomes clear that such traits are a type of psychological and moral illness—a kind of deviation in perception and thinking. Otherwise, a person who is healthy in spirit and soul would never be afflicted by such fancies and imaginations.

It goes without saying that Luqman’s intent is not merely the act of turning one's face away from people or walking insolently, but rather the struggle against all manifestations of arrogance and pride. However, since these traits manifest themselves first and foremost in ordinary daily movements, he has placed his finger on these specific manifestations.

In the next verse, he describes two other moral programs that are positive in nature—in contrast to the previous two which were negative—saying: "O my son! Observe moderation in your walking" (وَاقْصِدْ فِي مَشْيِكَ). "And also observe moderation in your speech and lower your voice, and do not shout" (وَاقْغُضْ مِنْ صَوْتِكَ).
