"For the harshest of sounds is the braying of donkeys!" (إِنَّ أَنْكَرَ الْأَصْوَاتِ لَصَوْتُ الْحَمِيرِ). [^1] [^2] In fact, in these two verses, two traits are forbidden and two traits are commanded:

The "prohibition" is against "self-superiority" and "self-conceit," one of which causes a person to be arrogant toward the servants of Allah, and the other causes a person to imagine themselves at the peak of perfection, thereby closing the doors of evolution upon themselves, even if they do not compare themselves with others.

[^1]: "Hamir" (حَمِير) is the plural of "himar" (حِمَار), meaning donkey. [^2]: "Ankar" (أَنْكَرَ) is a superlative (af'al al-tafdil), and although the superlative is not usually used for the object, this form has appeared rarely in the context of defects ("ankar" is the superlative of "munkar").

Although these two traits are often coupled and have a common root, they sometimes diverge.

The "command" is to observe moderation in "action" and "speech," for the emphasis on moderation in walking or the tone of voice is, in fact, by way of example.

Truly, one who possesses these four traits is a successful, happy, and victorious person—beloved among the people and dear in the presence of Allah.

It is noteworthy that there may be sounds in our environment more distressing than the braying of donkeys (such as the sound of certain pieces of metal scraping against each other, which makes one feel as if their flesh is crawling!). However, undoubtedly, these sounds are not general or universal. Furthermore, being "distressing" is different from being "ugliest." That which is truly the ugliest among the common sounds that humans hear is the braying of the donkey, to which the shouts and screams of the arrogant and the foolish have been likened.

The ugliness is not only in terms of the loudness and style of the sound but sometimes due to its being without reason. As some commentators have said, the sounds of other animals are often due to a need, but this animal sometimes screams without reason, without any need, and without any preamble, at any time! Perhaps for this reason, it is narrated in some traditions that whenever a donkey brays, it has seen a devil.

Some have said that the cry of every animal is a glorification (tasbih) of Allah except the sound of the donkey! In any case, beyond all these words, the ugliness of this sound among all sounds requires no debate.

And if we see in some traditions narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that this verse is interpreted as a sneeze uttered with a loud voice, or shouting while speaking, it is in fact the statement of a clear instance of it. [^1]

Key Points

1- The Etiquette of Walking

It is true that walking is a simple matter, but this very simple matter can reflect internal and moral states and occasionally be a sign of a person's personality. As we have said before, a person's psyche and character are reflected in the midst of all their actions, and sometimes a small act indicates a deep-rooted disposition.

Since Islam has paid attention to all dimensions of life, it has not neglected anything in this regard either.

In a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever walks upon the earth with insolence, the earth curses him, as do those beneath it and those above it!" [^2] Again, in another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "He forbade a man from being insolent in his walking and said: Whoever wears a garment and behaves arrogantly in it, Allah will cause him to sink from the brink of Hell and he will be a companion of Qarun, for he was the first who established pride and arrogance!" [^1] We also read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "Allah has made faith obligatory upon the limbs and members of man and distributed it among them: among these, He made it obligatory upon the feet of man that they should not walk toward disobedience and sin, and that they should step in the path of Allah's pleasure. Therefore, the Quran said: 'Do not walk insolently upon the earth...' and it also said: 'Observe moderation in your walking.'" [^2] In another narration, this incident is quoted from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he was passing through an alley and saw a madman whom people had surrounded and were looking at. He asked: "Why have these people gathered?" They replied: "Around a madman who is suffering from an epileptic fit."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله looked at them and said: "This is not a madman. Shall I inform you of the true madman?" They said, "Yes, O Messenger of Allah." He said: "The true madman is the one who walks with an arrogant gait, constantly looks at his own sides, and moves his sides along with his shoulders (arrogance and pride radiate from his entire being).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question. [^2]: Thawab al-A'mal and Amali al-Saduq (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 207). [^1]: Thawab al-A'mal and Amali al-Saduq (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 207). [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 28 (Chapter: Faith is distributed among all the limbs of the body).

This is the true madman, but the one you saw is a sick person!" [^1]

2- The Etiquette of Speech

In Luqman’s counsels, there was an allusion to the etiquette of speech, and in Islam, a vast chapter has been opened for this issue, including:

  • As long as speaking is not a necessity, silence is better than it. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Silence is a rest for the intellect." [^2]
  • In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, it is stated: "Among the signs of understanding (fiqh) are: knowledge, forbearance, and silence. Indeed, silence is one of the doors of wisdom." [^3]
  • But of course, in other traditions, it is emphasized: "In cases where speaking is necessary, the believer must never remain silent," "The Prophets were invited to speak, not to silence," and "The means of reaching Paradise and escaping from Hell is speaking at the right time." [^4]

3- The Etiquette of Social Interaction

As much as importance has been given in Islamic traditions by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام to the issue of humility, good character, gentleness in encounters, and the abandonment of harshness in social interaction, few things have been given such importance.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 76, p. 57. [^2], [^3], [^4]: Wasa'il, Vol. 8, p. 532.

The best and most eloquent evidence in this regard are the Islamic traditions themselves, a sample of which we review here:

  • A person came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Messenger of Allah, advise me." Among the things he advised him was: "Meet your brother with a cheerful face." [^1]
  • In another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Nothing is placed in the scale of a person on the Day of Judgment superior to good character!" [^2]
  • In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated: "Righteousness and good character inhabit the lands and increase lifespans." [^3]
  • It is also narrated from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: "The thing that most causes my Ummah to enter Paradise is the fear of Allah (Taqwa) and good character." [^4]
  • Regarding humility and modesty, we read from Ali عليه السلام: "The ornament of the noble is humility." [^5]
  • Finally, in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Humility is the root of every precious good and a high station. If humility had a language that people understood, it would speak of the realities of the hidden consequences... Whoever humbles himself for Allah, Allah ennobles him over many of His servants... And there is no worship for Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, that He accepts and is pleased with, except that its door is humility." [^1]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 74, p. 171. [^2], [^3], [^4]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Good Character, pp. 81-82. [^5]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 120. [^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 121.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُۥ ظَـٰهِرَةً وَبَاطِنَةً ۗ وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلَا هُدًى وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ (٢٠)
Do you not see that Allah has disposed for you whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth and He has showered upon you His blessings, the outward, and the inward? Yet among the people are those who dispute concerning Allah without any knowledge or guidance or an illuminating scripture. (20)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۚ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٢١)
When they are told, ‘Follow what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘No, we will follow what we found our fathers following.’ What! Even if Satan be calling them to the punishment of the Blaze? (21)
۞ وَمَن يُسْلِمْ وَجْهَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَقَدِ ٱسْتَمْسَكَ بِٱلْعُرْوَةِ ٱلْوُثْقَىٰ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْأُمُورِ (٢٢)
Whoever surrenders his heart to Allah and is virtuous, has certainly held fast to the firmest handle, and with Allah lies the outcome of all matters. (22)
وَمَن كَفَرَ فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ كُفْرُهُۥٓ ۚ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٢٣)
As for those who are faithless, let their faithlessness not grieve you. To Us will be their return, and We will inform them about what they have done. Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts. (23)
نُمَتِّعُهُمْ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ نَضْطَرُّهُمْ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (٢٤)
We will provide for them for a short time, then We will shove them toward a harsh punishment. (24)

Commentary

A Secure Support!

After concluding the ten exhortations of Luqman regarding the Origin (Allah), the Resurrection, the way of life, and social and moral programs, the Quran proceeds to mention Divine blessings to complete the lesson. This is intended to awaken the sense of gratitude in people—a gratitude that serves as the source of "Gnosis of Allah" (Ma'rifat Allah) and the motive for obeying His commands. [^1]

[^1]: Some commentators, such as Al-Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani and Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, consider the verses under discussion to be related to the verses prior to Luqman's testaments, which addressed the polytheists saying: "This is the creation of Allah; show Me what those besides Him have created." In the verses under discussion, He says: "Do you not see that Allah has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth?" However, the conclusion of this verse, the subsequent verses, and the narrations regarding its interpretation are more consistent with the general meaning of the verse.

Addressing all of humanity, He says: "Do you not see that Allah has subjected to your command whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, so that they move in the direction of your interests and benefit?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَوْا أَنَّ اللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُمْ، ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ).

The subjection of celestial and terrestrial beings for man has a broad meaning. It includes matters within his control and choice, which he employs for his benefit through his will—such as many earthly creatures—as well as matters not under human control but which Allah has commissioned to serve man, such as the sun and the moon.

Therefore, all beings are subjected to Allah's command for the benefit of humans, whether they are under human command or not. Thus, the "L" in "lakum" (for you) is the so-called "L of benefit." [^1] Then He adds: "Allah has lavished His favors upon you—both apparent and hidden" (وَ أَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُ ظاهِرَةً وَ باطِنَةً).

"Asbagha" is derived from the root "sabagh" (rhyming with sabr), which originally refers to a wide, spacious, and complete shirt or coat of mail; subsequently, it has been applied to extensive and abundant blessings.

Regarding what is meant by "apparent" and "hidden" blessings in this verse, commentators have discussed much.

Some consider "apparent" blessings to be those that no one can deny,

[^1]: Regarding the subjection of beings for man, we had further discussions in Volume 10, under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd, page 120.

such as creation, life, and various types of sustenance; and they consider "hidden" blessings to refer to matters that cannot be perceived without careful reflection and study (such as many spiritual powers and constructive instincts).

Some have counted "apparent" blessings as the external limbs and "hidden" blessings as the heart.

Others have considered "apparent" blessings to be the beauty of the face, upright stature, and health of the limbs, while "hidden" blessings are the "Gnosis of Allah."

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that Ibn Abbas asked him about this, and he replied: "O Ibn Abbas! The apparent blessing is Islam, your complete and orderly creation by your Lord, and the sustenances He has bestowed upon you. As for the hidden blessing, it is the concealing of your ugly deeds and not disgracing you before the people." [^1] In another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "The manifest blessing is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Gnosis of Allah and Monotheism (Tawhid) which the Prophet brought; and the hidden blessing is the Wilayah (guardianship) of us, the Ahl al-Bayt, and the covenant of friendship with us." [^2] In reality, however, there is no contradiction between these interpretations; each expresses one of the clear instances of apparent and hidden blessings without limiting its broad concept.

At the end of the verse, it speaks of those who show ingratitude for the great Divine blessings that surround man from within and without, and who rise to dispute and contend against the Truth. It says: "And of the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge or guidance or an enlightening Book" (وَ مِنَ النَّاسِ مَنْ يُجادِلُ فِي اللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَ لا هُدىً وَ لا كِتابٍ مُنِيرٍ).

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Instead of recognizing the Giver of all those manifest and hidden blessings, they turn toward polytheism and denial out of ignorance and stubbornness.

What is the difference between "knowledge" ('ilm), "guidance" (huda), and an "enlightening Book" (kitab munir)?

Perhaps the best explanation is that "knowledge" refers to perceptions that man understands through his own intellect and reason; "guidance" refers to Divine and celestial teachers and leaders and scholars who can take his hand on this path and lead him to the intended destination; and "enlightening Book" refers to the Heavenly Books which, through revelation, make the heart and soul of man radiant.

In truth, this stubborn group neither possesses knowledge themselves, nor do they follow a guide or leader, nor do they seek help from Divine revelation. Since the path of guidance is restricted to these three matters, by abandoning them, they have been drawn into the wilderness of misguidance and the realm of devils.

The next verse points to the weak and flimsy logic of this misguided group, saying: "When it is said to them, 'Follow what Allah has sent down,' they say: 'No, we follow that upon which we found our fathers!'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اتَّبِعُوا ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ قالُوا بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ ما وَجَدْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا).

Since following ignorant and deviant ancestors is not part of any of the three guidance-giving paths mentioned above, the Quran refers to it as a satanic path, saying: "Even if Satan were inviting them to the punishment of the Blaze?!" (أَ وَ لَوْ كانَ الشَّيْطانُ يَدْعُوهُمْ إِلى‌ عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ) [^1]

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 17; p. 66

[^1]: Commentators usually take "law" here as conditional, with its response omitted, implied as: "Even if Satan were inviting them to the punishment of the Blaze, would they still follow him?"

In reality, the Quran here strips away the cover of "following the tradition of ancestors"—which has a deceptive appearance—and reveals the true face of their action, which is following Satan on the path to the fire of Hell.

Indeed, the leadership of Satan alone is enough for a person to oppose it, even if it were wrapped in invitations to the truth (which is certainly a deceptive cover). Similarly, the invitation to the fire of Hell is alone sufficient for opposition, even if the inviter were unknown. Now, if the inviter is Satan and his invitation is toward the blazing fire of Hell, the duty is clear.

Would any rational person abandon the invitation of the Divine Prophets toward Paradise and follow the invitation of Satan toward Hell?!

Then, it proceeds to describe the state of two groups—the pure believers and the polluted disbelievers—comparing them with each other and with what was stated regarding the followers of Satan and the blind imitators of ancestors. It says: "Whoever submits his heart and soul to Allah and bows his head in submission and obedience at the threshold of the Lord while being a doer of good, has indeed grasped the most trustworthy handhold" (وَ مَنْ يُسْلِمْ وَجْهَهُ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَقَدِ اسْتَمْسَكَ بِالْعُرْوَةِ الْوُثْقى‌). The meaning of "submitting one's face to Allah" is actually an allusion to complete attention with one's entire being toward the Pure Essence of the Lord, because the "face" (wajh), being the most noble part of the body and the center of the most important human senses, is used as a metaphor for his essence.

The expression "هُوَ مُحْسِنٌ" (while he is a doer of good) is an instance of mentioning righteous deeds after faith.

Grasping the "trustworthy handhold" is a subtle metaphor for the fact that for man to be

saved from the depths of the valley of materialism and to ascend to the highest peaks of gnosis, spirituality, and soulfulness, he needs a firm and reliable means. This means is nothing but faith and righteous deeds; anything else is decayed, breakable, and a cause for fall and death. Furthermore, what remains is this means, while everything else is fleeting and perishable.

Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "And to Allah belongs the outcome of all matters" (وَ إِلَى اللَّهِ عاقِبَةُ الْأُمُورِ).

In a hadith narrated in Tafsir al-Burhan through Sunni chains from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام from the Noble Prophet of Islam, it is stated: "After me, there will be a dark and gloomy fitna (trial); the one who is saved from it is he who grasps the Trustworthy Handhold (al-'Urwat al-Wuthqa)." It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, what is the Trustworthy Handhold?" He said: "The Wilayah of the Master of Successors." It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, who is the Master of Successors?" He said: "The Commander of the Faithful (Amir al-Mu'minin)." It was asked: "O Messenger of Allah, who is the Commander of the Faithful?" He said: "The Master of the Muslims and their Imam after me." Again, to get a more explicit answer, they asked: "Who is he?" He said: "My brother, Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام." [^1] Other narrations have also been cited in this regard stating that the "Trustworthy Handhold" refers to the love of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, or the love of the Progeny of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, or the Imams from the descendants of Husayn عليه السلام. [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 279, under the verse being discussed.

[^2]: For further explanation, please refer to Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, pp. 278 and 279.

We have said many times that these interpretations express clear instances and do not contradict other instances such as Monotheism (Tawhid), piety (Taqwa), and the like.

Then, He describes the state of the second group, saying: "And whoever disbelieves and denies these clear truths, let not his disbelief grieve you" (وَ مَنْ كَفَرَ فَلا يَحْزُنْكَ كُفْرُهُ‌). For you have performed your duty well; it is he who wrongs himself. Such expressions, which appear repeatedly in the Holy Quran, show that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله suffered greatly when he observed a group of ignorant and stubborn people abandoning the path of Allah despite all these clear proofs and obvious signs, and heading into misguidance. He would become so grieved and sorrowful that Allah consoles him many times; such is the way of a compassionate leader.

Furthermore, do not be concerned that a group in this world, despite their disbelief and oppression, enjoys Divine blessings and is not seized by punishment, for it is not too late: "To Us is their return, and We shall inform them of what they did and its bitter and ominous consequences" (إِلَيْنا مَرْجِعُهُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا).

Not only are We aware of their deeds, but We are also aware of their intentions and the secrets within their hearts, "for Allah is Knowing of what is within the breasts" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

This expression—that Allah will inform people of their deeds on the Day of Resurrection or inform them of that wherein they differed—has been revealed in many verses of the Holy Quran. Considering that "nunabbi'ukum" is from the root "naba'", and "naba'"

according to what Raghib has stated in al-Mufradat, refers to news that has important content and benefit and is explicit, clear, and free from any falsehood, it becomes evident that these expressions indicate that Allah will perform such an exposure of human deeds on the Day of Resurrection that no room for objection or denial will remain for anyone. What people do in this world and often consign to oblivion, He will manifest in every detail and prepare for reckoning and recompense.

Even what passes through the heart of man, which no one but Allah knows, He will point out to their owners!

Then He adds: let their enjoyment of worldly life not amaze you, "We let them enjoy a little of the worldly provision—and worldly provision, whatever it may be, is little and insignificant—then We shall drive them by necessity to a severe punishment," a continuous and painful punishment (نُمَتِّعُهُمْ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ نَضْطَرُّهُمْ إِلى‌ عَذابٍ غَلِيظٍ). This expression may indicate that they should not imagine they are outside the grasp of Allah's power in this world; He Himself wishes to leave them free for testing, for the completion of proof, and for other purposes. This "little provision" given to them is also from Him. How different is the state of this group—who are humiliated and driven by necessity to the thick Divine punishment—from those whose entire being is at Allah's disposal, who have grasped the Trustworthy Handhold, who live purely and do good in this world, and who are blessed in the proximity of Divine Mercy in the Hereafter.

Ayahs

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٥)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah!’ But most of them do not know. (25)
لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٦)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth. Indeed Allah is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable. (26)
وَلَوْ أَنَّمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلَـٰمٌ وَٱلْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ مَّا نَفِدَتْ كَلِمَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٢٧)
If all the trees on the earth were pens, and the sea replenished with seven more seas [were ink], the words of Allah would not be spent. Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (27)
مَّا خَلْقُكُمْ وَلَا بَعْثُكُمْ إِلَّا كَنَفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٢٨)
Your creation and your resurrection are not but as of a single soul. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (28)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِىٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٢٩)
Have you not regarded that Allah makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night; and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term, and that Allah is well aware of what you do? (29)
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٣٠)
That is because Allah is the Reality, and whatever they invoke besides Him is nullity, and because Allah is the All-exalted, the All-great. (30)

Commentary

Ten Attributes of the Lord

In the six preceding verses, a collection of Divine attributes has been described, which in reality expresses ten major attributes or ten of the Asma al-Husna (The Most Beautiful Names):

Ghani (Self-Sufficient), Hamid (Praiseworthy), Aziz (Almighty), Hakim (All-Wise), Sami’ (All-Hearing), Basir (All-Seeing), Khabir (All-Aware), Haqq (The Truth), Ali (The Most High), and Kabir (The Most Great).

From one perspective, the first verse speaks of Allah’s "Creatorship," the second discusses His "Absolute Ownership," the third addresses His "Infinite Knowledge," and the fourth and fifth verses speak of His "Boundless Power."

In the final verse, it concludes that He who possesses these attributes is "The Truth" (Haqq), and everything besides Him is falsehood, naught, and vanity.

With this brief overview in mind, let us return to the detailed explanation of the verses:

First, He says: "If you ask them who created the heavens and the earth, they will certainly say, 'Allah'" (وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ).

This expression, which is also seen in other verses of the Quran (Surah Al-Ankabut 61-63, Az-Zumar 38, Az-Zukhruf 9), serves on one hand as evidence that the polytheists never denied the Oneness of the Creator (Tawhid al-Khaliqiyyah), and they could not attribute the act of creation to idols; they only believed in polytheism regarding worship and the intercession of idols.

On the other hand, it is proof of the primordial nature (fitrah) of Monotheism and the manifestation of this Divine light in the essence of all human beings.

Then He says, since they confess to the Oneness of the Creator: "Say: All praise belongs to Allah, who is the Creator of all things, not the idols who are themselves His creatures; but most of them do not know and do not understand that worship must be exclusive to the Creator of the universe" (قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ).

Then He addresses the issue of Divine "Ownership," for once Creatorship is established, there is no need for further proof of His Ownership. He says: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth" (لِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ). It is evident that He who is the "Creator" and "Owner" is also the Disposer of the affairs of the world. In this way, the three dimensions of Monotheism (Monotheism in Creatorship, Monotheism in Ownership, and Monotheism in Lordship) are proven.

And He who is such is independent of all things and worthy of every kind of praise. For this reason, at the end of the verse, He adds: "Indeed, Allah is the Self-Sufficient, the Praiseworthy" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ الْحَمِيدُ).

He is Absolute in His Self-Sufficiency and Praiseworthy from every aspect, because every blessing in the world returns to Him, and whoever possesses anything has it from Him. The treasuries of all goodness are in His hand, and this is living proof of His Self-Sufficiency.

Since "Praise" (Hamd) means extolling someone for a good deed performed by choice, and every goodness we see in the world is from the Lord, then all praise belongs to Him. Even if we praise the beauty of a flower, describe the attraction of celestial loves, or honor the greatness of a self-sacrificing martyr’s work, in reality, we are still praising Him, for that beauty, that attraction, and that greatness are also from Him. Thus, He is "The Absolutely Praiseworthy."

The next verse provides a depiction of Allah’s infinite knowledge, visualized through a very expressive and eloquent example: Beforehand, we deem it necessary to mention that according to what is stated in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim: "A group of Jews, when they questioned the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) regarding the nature of the soul (Ruh), and the Quran replied to them: قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي وَمَا أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا ('Say: The soul is by the command of my Lord, and you have not been given of knowledge except a little'), this statement was hard for them to bear. They asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) if this applied only to them. He replied: 'No, it includes everyone (even us).'"

But they added: "O Muhammad! You think you have little knowledge regarding yourself while the Quran has been given to you, and to us the Torah; and in your Quran it says: 'He who is given wisdom is given much good.' These statements are inconsistent!"

At this point, the verse وَلَوْ أَنَّ مَا فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلَامٌ... (the verse under discussion) was revealed, clarifying that no matter how vast human knowledge may be, it is nothing more than an insignificant atom compared to Allah’s knowledge, and what seems "much" to you is very "little" to Allah [1]. We have mentioned a similar narration through another chain under verse 109 of Surah Al-Kahf.

In any case, the Holy Quran describes Allah’s infinite knowledge as follows:

"And if whatever trees are on the earth were pens and the sea [were ink], replenished thereafter by seven [more] seas, the words of Allah would not be exhausted. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise" (وَلَوْ أَنَّ مَا فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلَامٌ وَالْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ مَا نَفِدَتْ كَلِمَاتُ اللَّهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

"Yamudduhu" is from the root "midad," meaning ink or the colored substance used for writing. Originally, it is derived from "madd," meaning to stretch or extend, because lines are created by the stretching of the pen across the paper.

Some commentators have cited another meaning for it, which is the oil poured into a lamp that causes it to shine; both meanings actually return to the same root.

"Kalimat" is the plural of "kalimah" (word) and originally refers to the sounds a human uses to speak. Later, it was applied in a broader sense to anything that can express a concept. Since the various creatures of this world each manifest the Pure Essence of Allah and His knowledge and power, every being is referred to as a "Word of Allah" (Kalimatullah).

[1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 279.

This term is used especially for more noble and magnificent beings, as we read regarding the Messiah (عليه السلام) in verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa: إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُ (a similar meaning appears in verse 45 of Surah Al-Imran).

Consequently, "Words of Allah" is used to mean the knowledge and wisdom of the Lord.

Now we must reflect deeply: to write down all the information of one human, sometimes a single pen with a bit of ink suffices; it is even possible for other humans to record their entire collective knowledge with that same pen. But the Quran says: "If all the trees on earth were pens"—we know that from a single massive tree, thousands or even millions of pens can be made from its trunk and branches. Considering the vast volume of trees on earth and the forests covering many mountains and plains, and the number of pens that could be produced from them; and likewise, if all the oceans on earth—which cover approximately three-quarters of the globe with great depth—were to become ink, what an extraordinary situation for writing it would create, and how much science and knowledge could be written with it.

Especially considering the addition of seven other seas to it, each equivalent to all the oceans on earth, and particularly noting that the number "seven" here does not denote a specific count but is for "multiplicity" (takthir), referring to countless seas, it becomes clear how vast the scope of Allah’s knowledge is. Even then, all of these would be exhausted, yet His knowledge would not end.

Can there be a more fascinating or beautiful depiction of infinity than this? This number is so vivid and eloquent that it carries the waves of human thought into boundless and unlimited horizons, leaving one drowned in wonder and awe.

Given this eloquent and clear explanation, man feels that his knowledge compared to what is in Allah’s knowledge is like a zero against infinity. It is fitting only to say: "My knowledge has reached the point where I have realized my own ignorance." Even the comparison of a drop to the ocean seems inadequate to express this reality.

Among the subtle points observed in the verse is that "shajarah" (tree) appears in the singular, while "aqlam" (pens) appears in the plural, to indicate the vast number of pens that can be produced from a single tree with all its trunk and branches.

Also, the expression "al-bahr" (the sea) is singular with the "generic definite article" (alif-lam al-jins) to include all the oceans on earth, especially since all the world's oceans are connected and are effectively one vast sea.

Interestingly, regarding "pens," it does not speak of additional or backup pens, but regarding the oceans, it mentions seven other seas. This is because, during writing, the pen is consumed very little, while what is consumed most is the ink.

The choice of the word "sab'" (seven) in the Arabic language for multiplicity perhaps stems from the fact that the ancients considered the number of celestial bodies in the solar system to be seven (and indeed, what is visible to the naked eye today in the solar system is no more than seven bodies). Considering that a "week" as a complete cycle of time is no more than seven days, and the entire earth was divided into seven regions called the "seven climes," it becomes clear why seven is used as a complete number among units and to express multiplicity [1].

[1]: We have also discussed "The Infinite Knowledge of the Lord" in Vol. 12 under verse 109 of Surah Al-Kahf (p. 574).

After mentioning the infinite knowledge of the Lord, the verse speaks of His boundless power, saying: "Your creation and your resurrection are but as a single soul. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing" (مَا خَلْقُكُمْ وَلَا بَعْثُكُمْ إِلَّا كَنَفْسٍ وَاحِدَةٍ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ). Some commentators have said that a group of the Quraysh disbelievers, out of amazement and deeming it improbable, said regarding the Resurrection: "Allah created us in different forms and through various stages; one day we were a drop of semen, then we became a clot, then a lump of flesh, and then gradually we took various forms. How can Allah give all of us a new creation in a single hour?"

The verse under discussion was revealed to answer their claim. In reality, they were oblivious to the fact that concepts such as "hard" and "easy," or "small" and "large," are conceivable for beings like us who have limited power. However, before the infinite power of the Truth, all are equal. The creation of one human is no different from the creation of all humans, and the creation of a being in an instant or over long years is the same in the presence of His power.

If the amazement of the Quraysh disbelievers was about how these different natures, various shapes, and diverse personalities—especially after the human body has turned to dust and the dust has scattered and mixed—could possibly be separated and each return to its place, the answer is provided by the infinite knowledge and boundless power of Allah.

He has established such relationships among beings that a single unit is like a collective, and a collective is like a single unit.

Fundamentally, the cohesion and interconnectedness of this universe are such that every multiplicity within it returns to unity, and the creation of the totality of mankind follows the same principles as the creation of a single human.

And if their amazement was at the shortness of time—how the stages a human undergoes from the state of a sperm to youth over many years could be traversed in short moments—the answer is again provided by the power of the Lord. In the world of living beings, we see human infants who must take a long time to learn to walk well or be able to use any type of food, while we see chicks that, as soon as they hatch and are born, stand up, walk, and even eat food without needing the mother. These show that such matters have no impact on the power of Allah.

The mention of Allah being "Hearing" and "Seeing" at the end of this verse might be a response to another objection from the polytheists: assuming all humans with their diverse creations and characteristics are resurrected at a specific hour, how are their deeds and words accounted for? Deeds and words are things that vanish after existing.

The Quran answers: Allah is Hearing and Seeing; He has heard all their words and seen all their deeds (furthermore, absolute annihilation has no meaning in this world; rather, their deeds and words will always exist).

Beyond this, the phrase is a warning to these excuse-seekers that Allah is not unaware of your conversations intended to poison public opinion, and He is even aware of what you hold in your hearts and have not uttered.

The next verse is another emphasis and explanation of Allah’s vast power. Addressing the Prophet, it says: "Do you not see that Allah causes the night to enter the day and causes the day to enter the night?" (أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ النَّهَارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ).

And do you not see that "He has subjected the sun and the moon [to the service of humans]"? (وَسَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ).

"Each running its course for a specified term" (كُلٌّ يَجْرِي إِلَى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى).

"And that Allah is All-Aware of what you do?" (وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ).

"Wuluj" originally means "entry." Entering the night into the day and the day into the night may refer to the gradual increase and shortening of night and day throughout the year, which gradually subtracts from one and imperceptibly adds to the other, so that the four seasons appear with their unique characteristics and blessed effects. (Only at two points on earth is this gradual change and the four seasons absent: one is the true North and South Poles, where it is six months of night and six months of day throughout the year; the other is the narrow and precise line of the Equator, where night and day are equal all year round).

Or it may refer to the fact that the transformation of night into day and day into night, due to the existence of the Earth's atmosphere, does not occur suddenly so as to place humans and all living creatures in various dangers. Instead, the first rays of the sun from the time of dawn penetrate the depths of darkness, gradually gaining more influence until they cover the entire sky—exactly the opposite of what happens at the end of the day and the entry of the night.

This gradual, perfectly regular, and calculated transition is among the manifestations of Allah’s power. Of course, these two interpretations are not contradictory and may both be intended in the meaning of the verse.

Regarding the subjection (taskhir) of the "sun" and "moon" and other celestial bodies for humans—as we have said before—it means subjection in the way of serving man. In other words, the lam in "sakhara lakum" is the lam of benefit, not the lam of exclusive possession. This expression appears in the Holy Quran regarding the sun, moon, night, day, rivers, seas, and ships. All of these express the greatness of the human personality and the vastness of Allah’s blessings upon him, such that all creatures of the earth and sky are, by Allah’s command, wandering and obedient to him. Yet, it is not fair that he should not obey [1]. The phrase "كُلٌّ يَجْرِي إِلَى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى" indicates that this calculated and precise system will not continue forever; it has an end and a conclusion that coincides with the end of the world, the very thing mentioned in Surah At-Takwir:

إِذَا الشَّمْسُ كُوِّرَتْ وَإِذَا النُّجُومُ انْكَدَرَتْ: "When the sun is wrapped up [in darkness] and when the stars fall, losing their light..."

The connection of the phrase "أَنَّ اللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ" with what we said above becomes clear, for the God who has employed the magnificent sun and moon with such precise calculation, and enters night and day into one another with that specific order for thousands and millions of years—how is it possible for such a Lord to remain unaware of the deeds of humans? Yes, He knows both the deeds and the intentions and thoughts.

[1]: Regarding "the subjection of the sun, moon, and other creatures for man," we had a detailed discussion in Vol. 10 under verse 2 of Surah Ar-Ra'd (p. 120) and verse 32 of Surah Ibrahim (p. 350).

In the final verse under discussion, as a comprehensive and general conclusion, He says:

"That is because Allah is the Truth, and that what they invoke besides Him is falsehood, and that Allah is the Most High, the Most Great" (ذَلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الْبَاطِلُ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ) [1]. The sum of the discussions in the previous verses regarding the Creatorship, Ownership, Knowledge, and Infinite Power of the Lord proved these matters: that "The Truth" is He alone, and everything besides Him is vanishing, false, limited, and needy. And "The Most High and The Most Great," who is superior to everything and beyond description, is His Pure Essence. As the poet said: Be aware that everything except Allah is false, And every blessing will inevitably vanish.

This statement can be expressed in philosophical terms as follows: "Truth" (Haqq) refers to the real and enduring existence. In this world, the real existence that is self-subsistent, stable, established, and eternal is only He. Everything else has no existence in its own essence and is the very essence of falsehood; they acquire their being through dependence on that Enduring Truthful Existence. Any moment He withdraws His gaze of grace from them, they vanish and disappear into the darkness of non-existence.

In this way, the more the connection of other beings to the Existence of Allah the Almighty increases, the more "truthfulness" they acquire.

In any case, as we have said, these verses are a collection of ten prominent attributes of Allah and ten of His Most Beautiful Names, containing strong and undeniable proofs for the falsehood of every kind of polytheism and the necessity of Monotheism in all stages of servitude.

[1]: The "ba" in "بِأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْحَقُّ," although it may initially seem to be the "ba of causality" (ba al-sababiyyah)—and perhaps for this reason some commentators like Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani took the content of this verse as the cause for the preceding matters—the context of the verses and the mention of the previous attributes (Creatorship, Ownership, Knowledge, Power, and their signs in the world of creation) clearly indicate that they were all witnesses for this conclusion. Therefore, the content of this verse is the result of the previous verses, not their cause.

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱلْفُلْكَ تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ لِيُرِيَكُم مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (٣١)
Have you not regarded that the ships sail at sea with Allah’s blessing, that He may show you some of His signs? There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]. (31)
وَإِذَا غَشِيَهُم مَّوْجٌ كَٱلظُّلَلِ دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ فَمِنْهُم مُّقْتَصِدٌ ۚ وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ (٣٢)
When waves cover them like awnings; they invoke Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him. But when He delivers them towards land, [only] some of them remain unswerving. No one will impugn Our signs except an ungrateful traitor. (32)

Commentary

In the Whirlpool of Affliction!

Once again, in the two verses under discussion, the discourse concerns the blessings of Allah and the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) within the horizons and within the souls.

In the first verse, the argument from order is presented, and in the second verse, the discourse is about innate Monotheism (Tawhid al-Fitri); together, they complete the discussions that appeared in the preceding verses.

It says: "Do you not see that the ships sail on the sea by the grace of Allah, and by the blessing of His favor?" (أَ لمْ تَرَ أَنَّ الْفُلْكَ تَجْرِي فِي الْبَحْرِ بِنِعْمَتِ اللَّهِ) ^1 "The purpose is that He may show you some of the signs of His greatness" (لِيُرِيَكُمْ مِنْ آياتِهِ).

Indeed, "In these are signs for everyone who is very patient and deeply grateful" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ).

Undoubtedly, the movement of ships upon the surface of the oceans is the result of a collection of laws of creation:

  1. The regular movement of winds on one hand.
  2. The specific gravity of wood or the materials from which the ship is constructed on the other hand.
  3. The degree of the density of water on the third hand.
  4. And the pressure exerted by the water upon objects floating in it on the fourth hand.

Whenever a disturbance occurs in any of these matters, the ship either sinks to the bottom of the sea, capsizes, or remains wandering and bewildered in the middle of the ocean.

However, Allah, who willed that the expanse of the seas be the best highway for human travel and for transporting required materials from one point to another, has provided these conditions, each of which is one of His blessings.

The greatness of Allah's power on the surface of the oceans and the smallness of man in comparison to it is such that in the past, when only the power of wind was used for the movement of ships, if all the people of the world gathered to move a ship in the middle of the sea against the path of a heavy wind, they would not have the power.

And today, even though the power of massive engines has replaced the wind, the blowing of a storm is still so harsh and heavy that it displaces the largest ships and sometimes shatters them.

The fact that at the end of the verse, emphasis is placed on the attributes of "Sabbar" and "Shakur" (the very patient and the very grateful) is either because worldly life is a collection of "affliction" and "blessing," both of which are means of trial; steadfastness and patience in the face of harsh events, and gratitude for blessings, constitute the entirety of human duty.

Therefore, in a hadith narrated by many commentators from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Faith consists of two halves: one half is patience and the other half is gratitude." ^1 Or it is an indication that to perceive the magnificent signs of Allah in the expanse of creation, a motivation is necessary, such as gratitude toward the Benefactor coupled with patience and endurance for greater precision and curiosity.

After explaining the blessing of the movement of ships in the seas—which, both in the past and today, has been the largest and most useful means of transporting people and goods—it points to another aspect of this same issue, saying: "When they board a ship and are in the middle of the sea, and the sea becomes stormy, and mountain-like waves loom over them like clouds, they call upon Allah with sincere devotion" (وَ إِذا غَشِيَهُمْ مَوْجٌ كَالظُّلَلِ دَعَوُا اللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ).

"Zulal" is the plural of "Zullah" (rhyming with Qullah), and commentators have mentioned several meanings for it:

  • Raghib in Al-Mufradat says: "Zullah" means a cloud that casts a shadow, and it is mostly used in cases where something unpleasant is involved.
  • Some have considered it to mean a canopy or awning, derived from the root "Zill" (shadow).
  • Some have taken it to mean a mountain.

Although these meanings—in relation to the verse under discussion—do not differ greatly, given that the Quran repeatedly uses this word to mean overshadowing clouds, and considering that the expression "ghashiyahum" (covered them) is more compatible with the meaning of a cloud, this interpretation seems closer.

That is, the massive waves of the sea rise in such a way and surround them that it is as if a cloud has cast a shadow over them—a terrifying and horrifying shadow.

It is here that man, with all the apparent powers he has attracted and acquired for himself, sees himself as weak, insignificant, and helpless. His hands are cut off from everywhere; all ordinary and material means fail; no window of hope remains for him except the light that shines from within his soul and from the depths of his primordial nature (Fitrah).

It pushes aside the veils of negligence, illuminates his heart, and tells him: "There is Someone who can deliver you! The same One whom the waves of the sea obey, and for whom water, wind, and earth are subservient."

It is here that pure Monotheism (Tawhid) encompasses his entire heart, and he considers religion, faith, and worship to belong exclusively to Him.

Then it adds: "When Allah delivers them from this peril—the waves subside, and he reaches the shore of safety—the people become two groups: some take the path of moderation and remain committed and faithful to the covenant and promise they made with Allah in their hearts during those sensitive moments" (فَلَمَّا نَجَّاهُمْ إِلَى الْبَرِّ فَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ) ^1 but another group forgets everything, and once again the plundering army of polytheism and disbelief conquers the territory of their hearts.

A group of commentators has considered the above verse to be an allusion to the conversion to Islam of "Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl."

At the time of the Conquest of Makkah, since the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had granted a general amnesty to all people except four individuals, and had issued a death sentence only for these four—one of whom was Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl—stating that they should be eliminated wherever they were found (because they spared no effort in sabotage, malice, and crimes against Islam and Muslims), Ikrimah was forced to flee Makkah.

He came to the shore of the Red Sea and boarded a ship. In the sea, a dangerous gale caught him. The people on the ship said to one another: "Come, bid farewell to the idols and cling only to the grace of 'Allah,' for these gods of ours can do nothing!" Ikrimah said: "If nothing but Monotheism saves us at sea, it will not save us on land either. O Allah, I make a covenant with You that if You deliver me from this peril, I will go to Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and place my hand in his, for I find him to be forgiving and generous."

Ultimately, he was saved, came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and became a Muslim. ^1 It is recorded in Islamic histories that Ikrimah later joined the ranks of the sincere Muslims and drank the nectar of martyrdom in the events of the Battle of Yarmouk or Ajnadayn.

And at the end of the verse, it adds: "None deny Our signs except every treacherous ungrateful one" (وَ ما يَجْحَدُ بِآياتِنا إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ).

"Khattar" is derived from the root "khatr" (rhyming with chatr), meaning the breaking of a covenant.

This word is an intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah) because polytheists and sinners repeatedly turn toward Allah during hardships, make covenants and promises with Allah, and make vows; however, when the storm of events subsides, they break their covenants one after another and consign divine blessings to ingratitude.

In reality, "Khattar" and "Kafur" mentioned at the end of this verse are the opposites of "Sabbar" and "Shakur" mentioned at the end of the previous verse (ingratitude versus gratitude, and covenant-breaking versus patience and remaining true to one's word). This is because fulfilling a covenant is only possible for the patient; they are the ones who, when the light of innate faith is ignited within their souls, strive to ensure this divine light does not fade again and that veils and curtains do not fall over it.

^1: The "Baa" in "bi-ni'matillah" may be the "Baa of causality" or the "Baa of accompaniment," but the first possibility is more appropriate.

^1: Muqtasid is from the root qasd, meaning moderation in action and fulfillment of a covenant. ^1: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verse discussed—in "Usd al-Ghabah fi Ma'rifat al-Sahabah," Vol. 4, p. 5, the same event is mentioned with slight differences (Ikrimah with a kasrah on the 'ayn).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ وَٱخْشَوْا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا يَجْزِى وَالِدٌ عَن وَلَدِهِۦ وَلَا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جَازٍ عَن وَالِدِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۖ فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (٣٣)
O mankind! Be wary of your Lord and fear the day when a father will not atone for his child, nor the child will atone for its father in any wise. Indeed Allah’s promise is true. So do not let the life of the world deceive you, nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah. (33)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ وَيُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ ۖ وَمَا تَدْرِى نَفْسٌ مَّاذَا تَكْسِبُ غَدًا ۖ وَمَا تَدْرِى نَفْسٌۢ بِأَىِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌۢ (٣٤)
Indeed the knowledge of the Hour is with Allah. He sends down the rain, and He knows what is in the wombs. No soul knows what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul knows in what land it will die. Indeed Allah is all-knowing, all-aware. (34)

Commentary

The Vastness of Allah’s Knowledge

In these two verses, which are the final verses of Surah Luqman, first, as a summary of the previous exhortations, counsels, and the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Hereafter (Ma'ad), all of humanity is directed toward Allah and the Day of Resurrection. Then, they are cautioned against the arrogance arising from the world and the Devil.

Subsequently, the verses address the vast scope of Allah’s knowledge and its encompassment of all things.

He says: "O mankind, fear your Lord" (يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ).

"And fear a Day when no father will avail his son, nor will a son avail his father at all" (وَ اخْشَوْا يَوْماً لا يَجْزِي والِدٌ عَنْ وَلَدِهِ وَ لا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جازٍ عَنْ والِدِهِ شَيْئاً).

In reality, the first command is an orientation toward the Origin (Mabda'), and the second command is an orientation toward the Return (Ma'ad).

The first command awakens the power of self-vigilance in man, and the second command awakens the sense of reward and punishment. Undoubtedly, one who knows that an All-Aware and Informed Being sees, knows, and records all his actions, and on the other hand, that a court of justice will be established to examine every detail of them, such a person is less likely to be tainted by sin and corruption.

The phrase "لا يَجْزِي" (la yajzi) is derived from the root jaza. Linguistically, jaza has two meanings: one is to reward or punish in return for something (as it is said: jazahullahu khayra—may Allah reward him with good).

The other meaning is to suffice, to substitute, or to bear a burden, as seen in the verse under discussion: "لا يَجْزِي والِدٌ عَنْ وَلَدِهِ" (no father shall accept responsibility for his son's deeds, nor stand in his place, nor suffice for him).

It is possible that both meanings return to a single root, for reward and punishment also stand in place of the action and are proportionate to it (reflect on this).

In any case, on that Day, everyone will be so preoccupied with themselves and so entangled in the complexities of their own deeds that they will not attend to anyone else. Even father and son, who share the closest of bonds, will not think of one another.

This verse is similar to what appears at the beginning of Surah Al-Hajj regarding the Resurrection and its earthquake: "يَوْمَ تَرَوْنَها تَذْهَلُ كُلُّ مُرْضِعَةٍ عَمَّا أَرْضَعَتْ" (The Day you shall see it, every nursing mother will forget her nursling).

It is noteworthy that regarding the "father," the expression "لا يَجْزِي" (a present/future tense verb) is used, while regarding the "son," the expression "جازٍ" (an active participle/noun) is used. This difference in expression may be for the sake of literary variety, or it may point to the duty and responsibility of the son toward the father, as the active participle implies greater duration and repetition.

In other words, it is expected from paternal affection that he would, at least in some instances, bear the suffering of his child, just as he endured hardships for him in this world. Conversely, it is expected of the son to bear a greater portion of the father's hardships due to the extensive rights the father has over him. Yet, on that Day, neither of the two will alleviate the slightest problem for the other; all are preoccupied with their own deeds and lost in their own plight.

At the end of the verse, He warns humanity against two things, saying: "Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth, so let not the worldly life deceive you, and let not the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah" (إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ فَلا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا وَ لا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ).

In fact, corresponding to the two commands at the beginning of the verse, two prohibitions are seen here. For if the awareness of Allah and the fear of the Reckoning and Reward are awakened in man, there is no fear of deviation or pollution except through two paths: one is that the glitz and glamour of the world distort realities in his eyes and strip him of his power of discernment—for the love of the world is the root of all sins. The other is that the whisperings of the Devil deceive him, make him arrogant, and distance him from the Origin and the Return.

If these two paths of sin's entry are closed, no danger will threaten him anymore. Thus, these four instructions together provide a complete program for human salvation.

In the final verse of this Surah, in keeping with the discussion regarding the Day of Resurrection in the previous verse, mention is made of sciences that are exclusive to the Lord. It says: "Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ).

"And He sends down the rain" (وَ يُنَزِّلُ الْغَيْثَ) and is aware of all the details of its descent.

And also, "He knows what is in the wombs" (وَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْأَرْحامِ) with all their specific characteristics.

And "no soul knows what it will earn tomorrow" (وَ ما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ ما ذا تَكْسِبُ غَداً).

"And no soul knows in what land it will die" (وَ ما تَدْرِي نَفْسٌ بِأَيِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ).

"Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌ).

It is as if this entire verse is an answer to a question raised regarding the Resurrection—the same question that the polytheists of the Quraysh repeatedly asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "مَتى‌ هُوَ" (When will it be?) (Al-Isra: 51).

The Quran answers them by saying: No one but Allah knows the moment the Resurrection will occur, and according to the explicit text of other verses, Allah has hidden this knowledge from everyone: "إِنَّ السَّاعَةَ آتِيَةٌ أَكادُ أُخْفِيها" (Indeed, the Hour is coming—I almost conceal it) (Taha: 15), so that arrogance and negligence never overtake individuals.

Then it says: It is not only the matter of the Resurrection that is hidden from you; in your daily life and among the closest matters related to your life and death, there are many things of which you are unaware.

The timing of the descent of the life-giving drops of rain, upon which the lives of all living beings depend, is not known to any of you; you only discuss it through conjecture, estimation, and probability.

Likewise, no one is aware of the time of your inception in the mother's womb or the specific characteristics of the fetus.

Furthermore, the near future—meaning the events of your tomorrow—and the place of your death and departure from life are hidden from everyone.

You who have no information about these matters so close to your own lives, why should it be a surprise that you are unaware of the moment the Resurrection will occur? [1] It is narrated in the commentary Durr al-Manthur that a man named "Warath" from the tribe of "Bani Mazin" came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Muhammad! When will the Resurrection take place? Furthermore, our lands are suffering from drought; when will there be an abundance of blessings? Also, when I left, my wife was pregnant; when will she give birth? And I know what I have done today, but what will I do tomorrow? Finally, I know where I was born, but tell me, in which land will I die?" The above verse was revealed and stated that the knowledge of all these matters is with Allah. [1]

Key Points

1- Types of Arrogance and Deception!

The above verses warn that the glitz and glamour of worldly life should not deceive you. Then they speak of the deception of the Devil and declare a danger regarding it, for people are of several groups.

Some are so weak and powerless that merely observing the glitter of the world is enough to deceive and make them arrogant.

However, for some who have more resistance, the whisperings of the Devil must be added to it; the internal and external devils must join hands to deceive them. The expressions in the above verse are a warning to all these groups.

It is also necessary to mention that "Gharur" (on the pattern of jasur) means any deceptive entity. The fact that it has been interpreted as the Devil is, in reality, an identification of its most obvious instance; otherwise, every deceptive human, every deceptive book, every tempting position, and every entity that leads man astray is included in the broad meaning of this word—unless we broaden the concept of "Shaytan" (Devil) to include all of these.

Therefore, Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "'Gharur' is anything that makes a person arrogant and deceives him, whether it be wealth, status, lust, or the Devil; and it is interpreted as the Devil because he is the most wicked of deceivers."

And the fact that some have interpreted it as "the world" is because of the world's deceptiveness, as we read in Nahj al-Balagha: "It deceives, it harms, and it passes!" [1]

2- The Deceptiveness of the World

Undoubtedly, many manifestations of worldly life are arrogance-inducing and negligence-breeding, and sometimes they occupy man so much with himself that he becomes heedless of everything else.

For this reason, in some Islamic narrations from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked: "Which of the people is the most firm in opinion?" he replied: "He whom people do not deceive regarding himself, and whom the world does not deceive with its allurements." [2] Yet, despite this, within the various scenes of this deceptive world, there are eloquent scenes that express the instability of the world and the emptiness of its glitz in the clearest way—events that can awaken any intelligent person, and even make the unintelligent sharp-witted.

In a tradition, we read that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, heard someone disparaging the world and calling it deceptive. Ali عليه السلام turned to him and said: "O you who disparage the world while being caught in its deception and fooled by its falsehoods! Do you let yourself be deceived by the world and then disparage it? Are you the one who has a claim of crime against it, or does it have a claim against you? When did it lead you astray? Or when did it deceive you? Was it by the falling places of your fathers into decay, or by the resting places of your mothers under the soil...?!

Indeed, the world is a house of truth for him who is truthful with it, a house of safety for him who understands it, a house of richness for him who takes provision from it, and a house of admonition for him who takes heed from it. It is the mosque of the lovers of Allah, the place of prayer for the angels of Allah, the place of descent for the revelation of Allah, and the marketplace for the friends of Allah..." [1]

3- These Five Sciences are Exclusive to Allah

Beyond the fact that the tone of the above verse indicates that knowledge of the Resurrection, the descent of rain, the state of the fetus in the mother's womb, the things a person will do in the future, and the place of their death are in the hands of Allah and others have no access to them, the narrations regarding the commentary of the verse also emphasize this reality.

Among them, we read in a tradition: "Indeed, the keys of the Unseen are five, which none knows but Allah," and then he recited the above verse. [1] In another narration found in Nahj al-Balagha, we read: When Ali عليه السلام was informing of some future events, one of his companions said: "O Commander of the Faithful! Do you speak of the Unseen? And are you acquainted with the knowledge of the Unseen?"

The Imam عليه السلام laughed and said to that man, who was from the tribe of "Bani Kalb": "O brother of Kalb! This is not knowledge of the Unseen; it is rather learning from one who possesses knowledge (meaning the Prophet). Knowledge of the Unseen is only the knowledge of the Hour and what Allah the Sublime has enumerated in His saying: 'Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour...' Thus, Allah the Sublime knows what is in the wombs, whether male or female, ugly or beautiful, generous or miserly, wretched or blessed, and who will be fuel for the Fire and who will be a companion to the Prophets in the Gardens... This is the knowledge of the Unseen which no one knows but Allah, and other than that is knowledge which Allah taught His Prophet, and he taught it to me, and prayed for me that my chest might contain it and my heart might be filled with it." [2]

From this narration, it is clearly understood that the meaning of people's lack of awareness of these five matters refers to all their specific details. For example, if one day tools are placed at the disposal of mankind—and that day has not yet arrived—whereby they definitively know whether a fetus is male or female, this still does not create a problem. This is because "knowledge of the fetus" means knowing all physical characteristics, beauty or ugliness, health or illness, internal talents, scientific, philosophical, or literary tastes, and all other spiritual qualities and states—and this is not possible for anyone other than Allah.

Likewise, regarding when the rain will fall, which region it will cover, and exactly how much will fall in the sea and how much in the desert, valley, mountain, and wilderness—no one knows but Allah.

The same applies to the events of tomorrow, and the days after, and their characteristics and details.

From here, the answer to a question often raised becomes clear: it is said that we read in numerous histories and narrations that the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام and even some of the friends of Allah (Awliya) other than the Imams, informed of their own deaths or stated their burial places. For instance, in events related to Karbala, we have repeatedly read in narrations that the Prophet or the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام and previous prophets informed of the occurrence of the martyrdom of Imam Hussain عليه السلام and his companions in that land.

And in the book Usul al-Kafi, there is a chapter regarding the Imams' knowledge of the time of their passing.

The answer is: Awareness of some of these matters in the form of general knowledge—and that too through divine teaching—in no way contradicts the exclusivity of their detailed knowledge to the Pure Essence of Allah.

Furthermore, as we said, even this general knowledge is not inherent or independent; rather, it is accidental and educational, through divine teaching, to the extent that Allah wills and deems appropriate. [1] Therefore, we read in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that one of his companions asked: "Does the Imam know the Unseen?" He said: "No, but when he wills to know something, Allah teaches it to him." [2] Regarding the knowledge of the Unseen and how the Prophets and Imams are aware of it, many narrations have been recorded which we will discuss under the appropriate verses, but it is certain that among these, there are sciences that no one knows but Allah. [3]

O Lord! Enlighten the eye of our hearts with the light of sciences and knowledge! And grant us a portion of Your infinite knowledge. O Allah! Make it so that the glitz and glamour of this world do not deceive us, and that the deceptive Devil and our carnal desires do not make us arrogant. O Lord! Make us always aware of Your scientific encompassment over us, and let us not perform in Your presence what is contrary to Your pleasure.

End of Surah Luqman October 1983 (1st of Mehr, 1362) 15th of Dhu al-Hijjah, 1403

[1]: It is true that in the above verses, in the phrase "He sends down the rain," there is no mention of the matter of Allah's knowledge. For this reason, some have considered this sentence an exception among these phrases, intended to express Allah's power rather than His knowledge. However, the harmony of the five sentences with one another on one hand, and the numerous narrations in Nahj al-Balagha and other books on the other hand, serve as evidence that it also pertains to Allah's knowledge.
